print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old.")

# Expressions inside {}
print(f"Next year I will be {age + 1}.")

# Format specifiers
pi = 3.14159
print(f"Pi to 2 decimals: {pi:.2f}")   # Pi to 2 decimals: 3.14

# Alignment and padding
for i in range(1, 4):
    print(f"{i:3d} {i*i:4d}")        # right‑aligned columns
Common format specifiers: {value:width.precision type}
<ul>
• d – integer
• f – float
• .2f – 2 decimal places
• >, <, ^ – alignment (right, left, center)
• 0 – zero padding: {value:05d} → 00042
• , – thousand separator: {value:,d}
2. The format() Method (Python 2.6+)
More flexible than f‑strings when the format string is stored separately (e.g., templates).

print("Hello, {}!".format(name))
print("{0} is {1} years old, {0} likes Python.".format(name, age))
print("{name} is {age} years old.".format(name=name, age=age))

# Format specifiers similar to f‑strings
print("{:.2f}".format(pi))
3. Old %‑formatting (C‑style)
Legacy style, still seen in older code. Use %s for strings, %d for integers, %f for floats.

print("Name: %s, Age: %d" % (name, age))
print("Pi: %.2f" % pi)
Recommendation: Use f‑strings for most cases. Use .format() when the template is not known at compile time (e.g., from a config file). Avoid old % formatting in new code.
String Methods – Manipulating Text
Strings have many built‑in methods (since everything is an object). Some essential ones:

s = "  Hello, World!  "
s = s.strip()              # remove leading/trailing spaces -> "Hello, World!"
print(s.lower())           # "hello, world!"
print(s.upper())           # "HELLO, WORLD!"
print(s.replace("World", "Python"))  # "Hello, Python!"
print(s.split(", "))       # ['Hello', 'World!']
print("-".join(["a","b","c"]))  # "a-b-c"
print(s.startswith("Hello"))  # True
print(s.find("World"))         # index 7
Type Conversion (Casting)
Use built‑in functions to convert between types:

int("42")           # 42
float("3.14")       # 3.14
str(123)            # "123"
bool(0)             # False
bool(42)            # True

# Handle conversion errors gracefully
try:
    value = int(input("Enter number: "))
except ValueError:
    print("That's not a valid number!")
Complete Example – Personal Info Formatter

def display_profile(name, age, height, is_student):
    # f‑string with formatting
    print(f"Name: {name:15} Age: {age:3} Height: {height:.2f}m")
    status = "Student" if is_student else "Not a student"
    print(f"Status: {status}")

def main():
    # Get input (always string, need conversion)
    name = input("Name: ")
    age = int(input("Age: "))
    height = float(input("Height (m): "))
    student_input = input("Are you a student? (yes/no): ").strip().lower()
    is_student = student_input == "yes"

    print("\n--- Profile ---")
    display_profile(name, age, height, is_student)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Common Pitfalls
<ul>
• Floating‑point equality – never compare floats with ==. Use a tolerance: abs(a - b) < 1e-9.
• Mutable default arguments – not a primitive issue, but be careful later.
• String immutability – operations like s.upper() return a new string; they don't modify the original.
• Type mixing – "5" + 3 raises TypeError. Convert explicitly: int("5") + 3.
• Comparing with None – use is None or is not None, not ==.
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Python's primitive types: int (unbounded), float (double precision), bool (True/False), None.
• Strings are immutable sequences of Unicode characters.
• Use raw strings (r"...") to ignore escape sequences.
• Modern string formatting: f‑strings (f"...{var}...") are best for most cases.
• Format specifiers control width, precision, alignment, and thousands separators.
• Convert between types using int(), float(), str(), bool().

modules.title: Module 3: Core Data Structures (Lists & Tuples)
content: Python provides powerful built‑in data structures to store collections of data. Lists and tuples are ordered sequences that can hold any mix of types. Lists are mutable (you can change, add, or remove elements), while tuples are immutable (fixed once created). Understanding these structures is essential for almost any Python program.
Lists – The Workhorse Sequence
A list is created with square brackets []. It can contain any Python objects, even a mix of types.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
mixed = [1, "hello", 3.14, True]
empty = []

# Lists are zero‑indexed
print(fruits[0])   # apple
print(fruits[-1])  # cherry (negative index counts from end)
List Operations and Methods
<ul>
• Append: fruits.append("orange") – adds to end.
• Insert: fruits.insert(1, "blueberry") – inserts at index.
• Extend: fruits.extend(["grape", "kiwi"]) – adds multiple.
• Remove by value: fruits.remove("banana") – removes first occurrence.
• Remove by index: popped = fruits.pop(2) – removes and returns.
• Delete by index or slice: del fruits[0] or del fruits[1:3].
• Clear all: fruits.clear().
• Find index: fruits.index("cherry").
• Count occurrences: fruits.count("apple").
• Sort in‑place: fruits.sort() (for numbers/strings).
• Reverse: fruits.reverse().

numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
numbers.sort()          # [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
numbers.reverse()       # [5, 4, 3, 1, 1]
sorted_copy = sorted(numbers)  # returns new sorted list, original unchanged
Slicing – Extracting Sub‑Lists
Syntax: list[start:stop:step]. Start inclusive, stop exclusive.

letters = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']
print(letters[1:4])    # ['b', 'c', 'd']
print(letters[:3])     # ['a', 'b', 'c']
print(letters[2:])     # ['c', 'd', 'e']
print(letters[::2])    # ['a', 'c', 'e'] (step 2)
print(letters[::-1])   # ['e', 'd', 'c', 'b', 'a'] (reverse)
List Comprehensions – Concise Creation
A Pythonic way to build lists from other iterables.

squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]          # [0,1,4,9,...,81]
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]  # [0,2,4,...,18]
matrix = [[i*j for j in range(3)] for i in range(3)]  # 2D list
Tuples – Immutable Sequences
Tuples are like lists but cannot be changed after creation. Use parentheses () (though commas define the tuple).

point = (10, 20)
rgb = 255, 128, 0   # parentheses optional
single = (42,)      # trailing comma needed for single‑element tuple

# Access similar to lists
print(point[0])      # 10

# But cannot modify
point[0] = 30        # TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment
Why use tuples?
<ul>
• Immutable → can be used as dictionary keys (lists cannot).
• More memory efficient than lists.
• Communicate that the data should be constant.
Tuple Packing and Unpacking

# Packing
t = 1, 2, 3

# Unpacking
a, b, c = t          # a=1, b=2, c=3

# Swapping variables
x, y = 5, 10
x, y = y, x          # now x=10, y=5

# Extended unpacking (Python 3+)
first, *rest = [1,2,3,4]   # first=1, rest=[2,3,4]
Common Operations for Both Lists and Tuples
<ul>
• Concatenation: [1,2] + [3,4] → [1,2,3,4]
• Repetition: ["hi"] * 3 → ["hi","hi","hi"]
• Membership: 2 in [1,2,3] → True
• Length: len([1,2,3]) → 3
• Iteration: for item in my_list: print(item)
When to Use List vs. Tuple
<ul>
• List – collections that may change (e.g., user inputs, dynamic data).
• Tuple – fixed data (e.g., coordinates, RGB values, function return values).
Complete Example – Student Grade Manager

def main():
    grades = []  # list of tuples (student_name, grade)
    while True:
        name = input("Enter student name (or 'done'): ")
        if name.lower() == 'done':
            break
        grade = float(input(f"Enter grade for {name}: "))
        grades.append((name, grade))   # tuple packing

    # Sort by grade (using lambda)
    grades.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

    print("\n--- Grade Report ---")
    for name, grade in grades:          # tuple unpacking
        print(f"{name:15} {grade:.2f}")

    if grades:
        avg = sum(g for _, g in grades) / len(grades)
        print(f"\nClass average: {avg:.2f}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Lists are mutable, ordered sequences ([]). Use methods like append(), pop(), sort().
• Slicing creates new lists from subsets.
• List comprehensions provide a compact way to create lists.
• Tuples are immutable (()). Use them for fixed data and as dictionary keys.
• Tuple unpacking is elegant for multiple assignments.
• Both support iteration, membership, and indexing.

modules.title: Module 4: Lookups & Mapping (Dictionaries & Sets)
content: Lists and tuples store ordered sequences accessed by position (index). But often you need to look up values by a key – like a phone book (name → number). Dictionaries (dict) provide key‑value mapping with fast lookups. Sets store unique, unordered items and support mathematical operations like union and intersection. Both are essential for efficient data organization.
Dictionaries – Key‑Value Mapping
A dictionary is created with curly braces {} or dict(). Keys must be immutable (strings, numbers, tuples) and are unique. Values can be any type.

# Creating dictionaries
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 20, "grades": [85, 90]}
empty = {}

# Access values
print(student["name"])      # Alice
print(student.get("major"))  # None (instead of KeyError)

# Adding / updating
student["major"] = "CS"
student["age"] = 21

# Removing
del student["grades"]
age = student.pop("age")   # removes and returns value
last = student.popitem()    # removes and returns (key, value) pair
Iterating Over Dictionaries

for key in student:          # iterates over keys
    print(key, student[key])

for value in student.values():
    print(value)

for key, value in student.items():   # tuple unpacking
    print(f"{key} -> {value}")
Dictionary Comprehensions

squares = {x: x**2 for x in range(5)}   # {0:0, 1:1, 2:4, 3:9, 4:16}
names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]
lengths = {name: len(name) for name in names}
Common Dictionary Methods
<ul>
• dict.update(other_dict) – merges another dict (overwrites existing keys).
• dict.setdefault(key, default) – returns value if key exists, otherwise sets key to default and returns it.
• dict.fromkeys(keys, value) – creates dict with same value for all keys.

# Using setdefault for counting
word = "hello"
counts = {}
for ch in word:
    counts[ch] = counts.setdefault(ch, 0) + 1
# {'h':1, 'e':1, 'l':2, 'o':1}
Sets – Unordered Collections of Unique Elements
Sets are like dictionaries without values. Use {} or set() (empty set must be set(), not {} which is empty dict).

colors = {"red", "green", "blue"}
colors.add("yellow")
colors.remove("green")      # raises KeyError if missing
colors.discard("purple")    # no error if missing

# Set operations
A = {1,2,3,4}
B = {3,4,5,6}
print(A | B)   # union: {1,2,3,4,5,6}
print(A & B)   # intersection: {3,4}
print(A - B)   # difference: {1,2}
print(A ^ B)   # symmetric difference: {1,2,5,6}

# Check subset / superset
print({1,2}.issubset(A))    # True
print(A.issuperset({1,2}))  # True
Set Comprehensions
even_set = {x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0}   # {0,2,4,6,8}
Hashing – How Dict and Set Achieve Fast Lookups

Both dictionaries and sets use hash tables. When you insert a key, Python computes its hash (using hash()). The hash determines where to store the key (and value). Lookup is O(1) on average. That's why keys must be immutable – mutable objects can change their hash and break the table.
Practical Examples
Word Frequency Counter

text = "the cat and the hat and the cat"
words = text.split()
freq = {}
for word in words:
    freq[word] = freq.get(word, 0) + 1
print(freq)  # {'the':3, 'cat':2, 'and':2, 'hat':1}
Removing Duplicates from a List

original = [1,2,2,3,4,4,5]
unique = list(set(original))      # [1,2,3,4,5] (order not preserved)
Grouping Data with Defaultdict
The collections.defaultdict provides a default value for missing keys.

from collections import defaultdict

# Group words by their first letter
words = ["apple", "ant", "ball", "cat", "car"]
groups = defaultdict(list)
for w in words:
    groups[w[0]].append(w)
# groups['a'] = ['apple','ant'], groups['b']=['ball'], etc.
Complete Example – Student Course Registration

def main():
    # Dictionary: student_id -> set of course codes
    registrations = {}

    while True:
        print("\n1. Register student")
        print("2. Add course to student")
        print("3. Show student courses")
        print("4. Exit")
        choice = input("Choice: ")

        if choice == '1':
            sid = input("Student ID: ")
            if sid not in registrations:
                registrations[sid] = set()
                print(f"Student {sid} added.")
            else:
                print("Already exists.")

        elif choice == '2':
            sid = input("Student ID: ")
            if sid in registrations:
                course = input("Course code: ")
                registrations[sid].add(course)
                print(f"{course} added to {sid}.")
            else:
                print("Student not found.")

        elif choice == '3':
            sid = input("Student ID: ")
            if sid in registrations:
                courses = registrations[sid]
                if courses:
                    print(f"{sid} is registered for: {', '.join(courses)}")
                else:
                    print("No courses.")
            else:
                print("Student not found.")

        elif choice == '4':
            break

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Dictionaries map immutable keys to values; lookups are very fast (O(1) average).
• Use dict.get(key, default) to avoid KeyError.
• Iterate with .items() to get key‑value pairs.
• Sets store unique, unordered items. Great for deduplication and membership tests.
• Set operations (|, &, -, ^) mimic mathematical set algebra.
• Both rely on hashing; keys must be immutable.
• defaultdict and Counter (from collections) are powerful extensions.

modules.title: Module 5: Workflow Control & Exception Handling
content: Programs need to make decisions, repeat actions, and respond to errors. Python provides conditional statements (if, elif, else), loops (for, while), and exception handling (try/except) to control the flow of execution. Mastering these constructs allows you to write flexible and robust programs.
Conditional Statements – Making Decisions
if, elif, else
Python uses indentation to define blocks. No braces or endif.

age = 18
if age < 18:
    print("Minor")
elif age < 65:
    print("Adult")
else:
    print("Senior")
Ternary Operator (Conditional Expression)
status = "Adult" if age >= 18 else "Minor"
Truthiness
Any expression can be used as a condition. Falsy values: False, None, 0, empty sequences ('', [], (), {}, set()). Everything else is truthy.

name = input("Name: ")
if name:   # empty string is false
    print(f"Hello, {name}")
else:
    print("No name entered.")
Loops – Repeating Actions
for loop – Iterating Over Sequences

# Over list
for fruit in ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]:
    print(fruit)

# Over range
for i in range(5):      # 0,1,2,3,4
    print(i)

# With index using enumerate
colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]
for idx, color in enumerate(colors):
    print(f"{idx}: {color}")

# Over dictionary
for key, value in student.items():
    print(key, value)
while loop – Conditional Iteration

count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

# Infinite loop with break
while True:
    user_input = input("Type 'quit' to exit: ")
    if user_input == 'quit':
        break
    print(f"You typed: {user_input}")
break, continue, else on loops
<ul>
• break – exits the innermost loop.
• continue – skips the rest of the current iteration.
• else on loops – executes if the loop completed normally (without break).

# Search for prime
n = 17
for i in range(2, n):
    if n % i == 0:
        print(f"{n} is not prime")
        break
else:
    print(f"{n} is prime")   # executed only if no break
Exception Handling – Dealing with Errors Gracefully
Errors (exceptions) occur during execution: division by zero, file not found, invalid conversion. Instead of crashing, you can catch and handle them.
Basic try‑except

try:
    x = int(input("Enter a number: "))
    y = 10 / x
    print(f"Result: {y}")
except ValueError:
    print("That's not a valid integer!")
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Cannot divide by zero!")
else:
    print("No exceptions occurred.")   # optional
finally:
    print("This always runs.")         # cleanup (e.g., close file)
Catching Multiple Exceptions

try:
    risky_operation()
except (ValueError, ZeroDivisionError) as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")
except Exception as e:   # catch any other exception
    print(f"Unexpected: {e}")
Raising Exceptions
Use raise to signal an error condition.

def set_age(age):
    if age < 0 or age > 150:
        raise ValueError(f"Invalid age: {age}")
    return age
Creating Custom Exceptions

class NegativeNumberError(Exception):
    pass

def sqrt_positive(x):
    if x < 0:
        raise NegativeNumberError(f"Cannot take sqrt of {x}")
    return x ** 0.5
Practical Examples
Input Validation with Loop and Exception

def get_positive_int(prompt):
    while True:
        try:
            value = int(input(prompt))
            if value <= 0:
                print("Please enter a positive number.")
                continue
            return value
        except ValueError:
            print("Invalid input. Enter an integer.")

age = get_positive_int("Enter your age: ")
File Reading with Error Handling

filename = input("Enter file name: ")
try:
    with open(filename, 'r') as f:
        content = f.read()
        print(content)
except FileNotFoundError:
    print(f"File '{filename}' not found.")
except PermissionError:
    print("Permission denied.")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"An error occurred: {e}")
Complete Example – Simple Calculator with Robustness

def calculator():
    print("Simple Calculator")
    print("Operations: +, -, *, /")

    while True:
        try:
            num1 = float(input("First number: "))
            op = input("Operator (or 'q' to quit): ")
            if op.lower() == 'q':
                break
            num2 = float(input("Second number: "))

            if op == '+':
                result = num1 + num2
            elif op == '-':
                result = num1 - num2
            elif op == '*':
                result = num1 * num2
            elif op == '/':
                if num2 == 0:
                    raise ZeroDivisionError("Division by zero")
                result = num1 / num2
            else:
                print("Unsupported operator.")
                continue

            print(f"Result: {result}\n")

        except ValueError:
            print("Invalid number. Please enter numeric values.")
        except ZeroDivisionError as e:
            print(f"Error: {e}")
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            print("\nGoodbye!")
            break

if __name__ == "__main__":
    calculator()
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Use if/elif/else for decisions; indentation defines blocks.
• for loops iterate over sequences; while loops repeat while condition is true.
• break exits a loop; continue skips to next iteration; else on loops runs if no break.
• Exceptions allow graceful error handling. Use try/except to catch specific errors.
• Always include a finally block for cleanup (closing files, releasing resources).
• Raise exceptions with raise to signal errors in your functions.
• Do not catch Exception unless you have a good reason; catch specific exceptions first.

modules.title: Module 6: Functions & High-Order Paradigms
content: Functions are the primary way to reuse code and organize logic in Python. Beyond basic functions, Python supports first‑class functions (functions as objects), lambda expressions, closures, and decorators. These functional programming paradigms allow you to write concise, expressive, and modular code.
Defining and Calling Functions
Use def, a name, parentheses for parameters, and a colon. The body is indented.

def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

message = greet("Alice")
print(message)
Parameters and Arguments
<ul>
• Positional arguments – matched by order.
• Keyword arguments – specified by parameter name.
• Default parameters – assigned if argument not provided.
• Variable‑length arguments – *args (tuple) and **kwargs (dict).

def func(a, b=2, *args, **kwargs):
    print(f"a={a}, b={b}, args={args}, kwargs={kwargs}")

func(1)                     # a=1, b=2, args=(), kwargs={}
func(1, 3, 4, 5, x=10)     # a=1, b=3, args=(4,5), kwargs={'x':10}
func(b=20, a=10)            # keyword arguments can reorder
Return Values
A function without return returns None. You can return multiple values as a tuple.

def stats(numbers):
    return min(numbers), max(numbers), sum(numbers)/len(numbers)

lo, hi, avg = stats([1,2,3,4,5])
Scope and Lifetime
<ul>
• Local scope – variables defined inside a function.
• Global scope – variables defined at top level. Use global to modify them inside a function (discouraged).
• Nonlocal scope – for nested functions, use nonlocal to modify variables from outer (non‑global) scope.

x = 10   # global

def outer():
    y = 20   # enclosing
    def inner():
        nonlocal y   # allows modifying y from outer
        y += 1
        global x
        x += 1
    inner()
    print(y)   # 21
First‑Class Functions & Lambda
Functions are objects: you can assign them to variables, pass them as arguments, and return them from other functions.

def apply(func, value):
    return func(value)

def square(x):
    return x * x

result = apply(square, 5)   # 25

# Lambda – anonymous one‑line function
add = lambda a, b: a + b
print(add(3,4))             # 7

# Common use with sorted, filter, map
nums = [1,4,2,7,3]
sorted_nums = sorted(nums, key=lambda x: -x)  # descending

# filter
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))  # [4,2]

# map
squared = list(map(lambda x: x**2, nums))         # [1,16,4,49,9]
List Comprehensions vs. map/filter
List comprehensions are often more readable than map and filter with lambdas.

# Instead of list(filter(lambda x: x>0, nums))
positive = [x for x in nums if x > 0]

# Instead of list(map(lambda x: x*2, nums))
doubled = [x*2 for x in nums]
But map/filter are useful when you have an existing function.
Closures – Functions That Remember Environment
A closure is a nested function that captures variables from its enclosing scope, even after the outer function has returned.

def make_multiplier(factor):
    def multiplier(x):
        return x * factor   # factor is captured
    return multiplier

times2 = make_multiplier(2)
times3 = make_multiplier(3)
print(times2(5))   # 10
print(times3(5))   # 15
Decorators – Wrapping Functions
A decorator is a function that takes another function and extends its behavior without modifying it directly. Use @decorator_name syntax.

def timer(func):
    import time
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        end = time.time()
        print(f"{func.__name__} took {end-start:.4f}s")
        return result
    return wrapper

@timer
def slow_square(n):
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        total += i*i
    return total

slow_square(1000000)   # prints timing
Decorators with Arguments
Add another layer of nesting.

def repeat(times):
    def decorator(func):
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            for _ in range(times):
                func(*args, **kwargs)
        return wrapper
    return decorator

@repeat(3)
def say_hi():
    print("Hi!")

say_hi()   # prints "Hi!" three times
Higher‑Order Functions in Python
Python has built‑in higher‑order functions:
<ul>
• map(func, iterable) – applies function to each item.
• filter(func, iterable) – keeps items for which func returns True.
• functools.reduce(func, iterable) – cumulatively applies function (import from functools).
• sorted(iterable, key=func, reverse=bool) – sorts with key function.

from functools import reduce

numbers = [1,2,3,4]
product = reduce(lambda a,b: a*b, numbers)   # 24
Recursion (Briefly)
Python supports recursion, but it has a recursion limit (default ~1000). Use it for problems like tree traversal, not for deep loops.

def factorial(n):
    return 1 if n <= 1 else n * factorial(n-1)
Complete Example – Decorator for Input Validation

def validate_positive(func):
    def wrapper(x):
        if x < 0:
            raise ValueError(f"Argument must be non‑negative, got {x}")
        return func(x)
    return wrapper

@validate_positive
def square_root(x):
    return x ** 0.5

print(square_root(9))   # 3.0
# square_root(-1)       # raises ValueError
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Functions are defined with def. They can have default arguments, *args, and **kwargs.
• Scope: local, enclosing (nonlocal), global. Minimize use of global.
• Functions are first‑class objects: assign, pass, return them.
• Lambda expressions create small anonymous functions.
• Closures capture environment variables; useful for factories and callbacks.
• Decorators wrap functions to add behavior (logging, timing, validation). They use the @ syntax.
• Higher‑order functions like map, filter, reduce enable functional programming style.

modules.title: Module 7: Object-Oriented Execution (OOP)
content: Python is an object‑oriented language from the ground up. Everything in Python – numbers, strings, lists, functions – is an object. OOP allows you to model real‑world entities using classes (blueprints) and objects (instances). This module covers classes, attributes, methods, inheritance, polymorphism, and special methods (dunder methods).
Classes and Objects – The Basics
A class defines a new type. An object is an instance of that class.

class Dog:
    # Class attribute (shared by all instances)
    species = "Canis familiaris"

    # Constructor (initializer)
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name      # instance attribute
        self.age = age

    # Instance method
    def bark(self):
        return f"{self.name} says Woof!"

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} is {self.age} years old"

# Creating objects
buddy = Dog("Buddy", 3)
print(buddy.bark())      # Buddy says Woof!
print(buddy)             # Buddy is 3 years old (uses __str__)
The `self` Parameter
self refers to the current instance. It must be the first parameter of instance methods (though you can name it anything, self is convention).
Instance vs. Class Attributes
<ul>
• Instance attributes – unique to each object (defined inside __init__ with self).
• Class attributes – shared across all instances (defined directly in class body).

print(Dog.species)       # Access via class
print(buddy.species)     # Access via instance (looks up class if not in instance)
Instance Methods, Class Methods, Static Methods
<ul>
• Instance method – takes self, can modify instance state.
• Class method – takes cls, works on class level. Decorator: @classmethod.
• Static method – no special first parameter, like a regular function inside a class. Decorator: @staticmethod.

class MyClass:
    class_var = 0

    def __init__(self, value):
        self.value = value

    def instance_method(self):
        return f"Instance value: {self.value}"

    @classmethod
    def class_method(cls):
        cls.class_var += 1
        return f"Class var: {cls.class_var}"

    @staticmethod
    def static_method(x, y):
        return x + y

print(MyClass.class_method())   # Class var: 1
print(MyClass.static_method(5,3)) # 8
Inheritance – Reusing and Extending Behavior
A child class inherits attributes and methods from a parent class.

class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def speak(self):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement")

class Cat(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} says Meow!"

class Dog(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} says Woof!"

animals = [Cat("Whiskers"), Dog("Buddy")]
for a in animals:
    print(a.speak())   # Polymorphism: same interface, different behavior
super() – Calling Parent Methods

class Vehicle:
    def __init__(self, make, model):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model

class Car(Vehicle):
    def __init__(self, make, model, doors):
        super().__init__(make, model)   # call parent constructor
        self.doors = doors
Multiple Inheritance
Python supports multiple inheritance. Method Resolution Order (MRO) determines which parent method is called (C3 linearization).

class Flyer:
    def move(self):
        return "Flying"

class Swimmer:
    def move(self):
        return "Swimming"

class Duck(Flyer, Swimmer):
    pass

d = Duck()
print(d.move())   # Flying (first parent in list)
print(Duck.__mro__)  # shows method resolution order
Encapsulation – Name Mangling (Privacy Convention)
Python does not have true private members. By convention, prefix with _ for protected, __ for name mangling (makes it harder to accidentally access).

class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance):
        self.__balance = balance   # name mangled to _BankAccount__balance

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount > 0:
            self.__balance += amount

    def get_balance(self):
        return self.__balance

acc = BankAccount(100)
# print(acc.__balance)   # AttributeError
print(acc._BankAccount__balance)  # works but not recommended
Properties – Controlled Attribute Access
Use @property to define getters/setters without changing the interface.

class Temperature:
    def __init__(self, celsius):
        self._celsius = celsius

    @property
    def celsius(self):
        return self._celsius

    @celsius.setter
    def celsius(self, value):
        if value < -273.15:
            raise ValueError("Below absolute zero")
        self._celsius = value

    @property
    def fahrenheit(self):
        return self._celsius * 9/5 + 32

t = Temperature(25)
print(t.celsius)      # 25 (looks like attribute)
t.celsius = 30
print(t.fahrenheit)   # 86.0
Special (Dunder) Methods
Customize behavior of objects with methods like __str__, __repr__, __len__, __add__, etc.

class Vector:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

    def __add__(self, other):
        return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Vector({self.x}, {self.y})"

    def __len__(self):
        return 2

v1 = Vector(2, 3)
v2 = Vector(4, 5)
v3 = v1 + v2   # uses __add__
print(v3)      # Vector(6, 8)
print(len(v3)) # 2
Complete Example – Employee Management System

class Employee:
    _employee_count = 0

    def __init__(self, name, salary):
        self.name = name
        self.salary = salary
        Employee._employee_count += 1

    def give_raise(self, percent):
        self.salary *= (1 + percent / 100)

    @classmethod
    def from_string(cls, emp_str):
        name, salary = emp_str.split(",")
        return cls(name, float(salary))

    @staticmethod
    def is_workday(day):
        return day.weekday() < 5   # Monday=0, Friday=4

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name}: ${self.salary:.2f}"

class Manager(Employee):
    def __init__(self, name, salary, team_size):
        super().__init__(name, salary)
        self.team_size = team_size

    def give_raise(self, percent):
        # Managers get an extra 5% bonus
        super().give_raise(percent)
        self.salary *= 1.05

# Usage
emp = Employee("Alice", 50000)
emp.give_raise(10)
print(emp)

mgr = Manager("Bob", 80000, 5)
mgr.give_raise(10)
print(mgr)

print(f"Total employees: {Employee._employee_count}")
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Classes define blueprints; objects are instances.
• self refers to the current instance; cls for class methods.
• Inheritance promotes code reuse; super() calls parent methods.
• Encapsulation uses naming conventions (_, __) and properties.
• Special methods (dunder) allow your objects to behave like built‑in types.
• OOP in Python is flexible and powerful – use it to model complex systems.

modules.title: Module 8: Module Management & Pip Ecosystem
content: Python's real power comes from its vast ecosystem of third‑party libraries. A module is a file containing Python definitions and statements. A package is a collection of modules in a directory. pip is the package installer that lets you download and manage these libraries. This module teaches you how to organize your own code into modules and how to leverage the Python Package Index (PyPI).
What is a Module?
Any .py file is a module. You can import its functions, classes, and variables.

# Save as math_utils.py
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

def mul(a, b):
    return a * b

PI = 3.14159

# In another file
import math_utils
print(math_utils.add(5,3))   # 8
print(math_utils.PI)

# Alternative imports
from math_utils import mul, PI
print(mul(2,4))

# Import with alias
import math_utils as mu
print(mu.add(10,20))
The `__name__` Variable – Script vs. Import
When a module is run directly, __name__ is set to "__main__". When imported, it's the module name. This allows you to include test code.

# mymodule.py
def main():
    print("Module executed directly")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Packages – Organizing Modules into Directories
A package is a directory containing a special __init__.py file (can be empty).

my_package/
    __init__.py
    module_a.py
    module_b.py
    sub_package/
        __init__.py
        module_c.py
Importing:

import my_package.module_a
from my_package import module_b
from my_package.sub_package import module_c
The Python Package Index (PyPI) and pip
PyPI (https://pypi.org) hosts over 400,000 packages. pip is the command‑line tool to install them.

# Install a package
pip install requests

# Install specific version
pip install requests==2.28.1

# Upgrade
pip install --upgrade requests

# Uninstall
pip uninstall requests

# List installed packages
pip list

# Show package info
pip show requests

# Install from requirements file
pip install -r requirements.txt
Virtual Environments – Isolated Dependencies
Different projects may need different versions of the same package. Virtual environments solve this.

# Create a virtual environment (built‑in venv)
python -m venv myenv

# Activate (Windows)
myenv\Scripts\activate

# Activate (macOS/Linux)
source myenv/bin/activate

# Deactivate
deactivate
Now pip install will install packages only inside this environment.
Creating Your Own Package (for distribution)
Structure:

my_project/
    README.md
    setup.py
    mypackage/
        __init__.py
        core.py
        utils.py
Minimal setup.py:

from setuptools import setup, find_packages

setup(
    name="mypackage",
    version="0.1",
    packages=find_packages(),
    install_requires=[
        "requests>=2.0",
    ],
    author="Your Name",
    description="A sample package",
)
Install your package in editable mode: pip install -e .
Useful Third‑Party Libraries for Beginners
<ul>
• requests – HTTP requests (simpler than urllib).
• numpy – numerical computing.
• pandas – data analysis.
• matplotlib – plotting.
• flask – web framework.
• pytest – testing.
Importing from Different Locations – sys.path
Python searches for modules in directories listed in sys.path. You can add your own paths.

import sys
sys.path.append("/path/to/my/modules")
import mymodule
Complete Example – Using `requests` to Fetch Data

# First install: pip install requests
import requests

def get_weather(city):
    api_key = "your_api_key"  # get from openweathermap.org
    url = f"http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather?q={city}&appid={api_key}&units=metric"
    response = requests.get(url)
    if response.status_code == 200:
        data = response.json()
        temp = data['main']['temp']
        desc = data['weather'][0]['description']
        return f"{city}: {temp}°C, {desc}"
    else:
        return f"Error: {response.status_code}"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(get_weather("London"))
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Every .py file is a module; directories with __init__.py are packages.
• Use if __name__ == "__main__" to separate reusable code from script code.
• pip is the package installer – use it to tap into the PyPI ecosystem.
• Virtual environments keep dependencies isolated per project.
• You can distribute your own packages via setuptools and PyPI.

modules.title: Module 9: File I/O & JSON Parsing
content: Real programs need to persist data beyond a single run. Python provides built‑in functions for reading and writing files (text and binary). JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) has become the standard lightweight data interchange format, and Python's json module makes it trivial to parse and generate JSON. This module covers file handling and JSON manipulation.
Opening and Closing Files – The `open()` Function

file = open("data.txt", "r")   # read mode
content = file.read()
file.close()  # always close!

# Better: use `with` statement (auto‑closes)
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
    # no need to close manually
Common modes:
<ul>
• 'r' – read (default).
• 'w' – write (overwrites existing file).
• 'a' – append (writes to end).
• 'rb' / 'wb' – binary mode.
• 'r+' – read and write.
Reading Text Files

with open("poem.txt", "r") as f:
    # Read entire file as string
    whole = f.read()

    # Read line by line
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())

    # Read all lines into a list
    lines = f.readlines()

    # Read one line
    first_line = f.readline()
Writing Text Files

with open("output.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello, world!\n")
    f.writelines(["Line 2\n", "Line 3\n"])

with open("output.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("Appended line\n")
Binary Files – Reading and Writing Raw Bytes

data = bytes([0, 1, 2, 255])
with open("data.bin", "wb") as f:
    f.write(data)

with open("data.bin", "rb") as f:
    read_data = f.read()
    print(read_data)  # b'\x00\x01\x02\xff'
Working with CSV Files (Built‑in `csv` module)

import csv

# Writing
with open("data.csv", "w", newline="") as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerow(["Name", "Age", "City"])
    writer.writerow(["Alice", 30, "NYC"])
    writer.writerow(["Bob", 25, "LA"])

# Reading
with open("data.csv", "r") as f:
    reader = csv.reader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row)   # list of strings
JSON – The Universal Data Format
JSON is text that looks like Python dictionaries and lists. It is language‑independent.

import json

# Python object
person = {
    "name": "Alice",
    "age": 30,
    "city": "New York",
    "hobbies": ["reading", "cycling"]
}

# Serialize (Python → JSON string)
json_string = json.dumps(person, indent=2)
print(json_string)

# Write to file
with open("person.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(person, f, indent=2)

# Deserialize (JSON string → Python)
loaded = json.loads(json_string)
print(loaded["name"])

# Read from file
with open("person.json", "r") as f:
    restored = json.load(f)
    print(restored)
Handling JSON with Custom Objects
You need to provide custom encoder/decoder for non‑serializable objects (e.g., datetime, custom classes).

from datetime import datetime

def custom_encoder(obj):
    if isinstance(obj, datetime):
        return obj.isoformat()
    raise TypeError(f"Type {type(obj)} not serializable")

data = {"timestamp": datetime.now()}
json_str = json.dumps(data, default=custom_encoder)
print(json_str)
File Paths – Using `pathlib` (Modern Approach)
pathlib provides an object‑oriented interface to file system paths (Python 3.4+).

from pathlib import Path

p = Path("./data/settings.json")
print(p.exists())
print(p.is_file())
print(p.parent)   # ./data
print(p.stem)     # settings
print(p.suffix)   # .json

# Read/write directly
content = p.read_text()
p.write_text("new content")
Exception Handling with File Operations

try:
    with open("missing.txt", "r") as f:
        content = f.read()
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found!")
except PermissionError:
    print("Permission denied.")
except IOError as e:
    print(f"I/O error: {e}")
Complete Example – Todo List with JSON Storage

import json
from pathlib import Path

TODO_FILE = Path("todos.json")

def load_todos():
    if TODO_FILE.exists():
        with open(TODO_FILE, "r") as f:
            return json.load(f)
    return []

def save_todos(todos):
    with open(TODO_FILE, "w") as f:
        json.dump(todos, f, indent=2)

def add_task(todos, task):
    todos.append({"task": task, "done": False})
    save_todos(todos)

def list_tasks(todos):
    for i, item in enumerate(todos, 1):
        status = "✓" if item["done"] else " "
        print(f"{i}. [{status}] {item['task']}")

def mark_done(todos, index):
    if 0 <= index < len(todos):
        todos[index]["done"] = True
        save_todos(todos)

def main():
    todos = load_todos()
    while True:
        print("\n1. Add task\n2. List tasks\n3. Mark done\n4. Exit")
        choice = input("Choice: ")
        if choice == "1":
            task = input("Task: ")
            add_task(todos, task)
        elif choice == "2":
            list_tasks(todos)
        elif choice == "3":
            idx = int(input("Number: ")) - 1
            mark_done(todos, idx)
        elif choice == "4":
            break

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Use with open(...) as f for safe file handling (auto‑close).
• Read/write text with .read(), .write(); binary with 'rb'/'wb'.
• CSV files are handled by the csv module.
• JSON is the go‑to format for structured data; json.dump() and json.load() are your friends.
• pathlib makes path manipulation cleaner and cross‑platform.
• Always handle file exceptions (FileNotFoundError, PermissionError).

modules.title: Module 10: Final Deployment Project
content: This final module brings together everything you have learned – variables, data structures, control flow, functions, OOP, modules, file I/O, and JSON – to build a complete, deployable application. You will create a Personal Library Manager with a command‑line interface, persistent storage, and clean architecture. Then you will learn how to package and share your application.
Project Overview – Personal Library Manager
Features:
<ul>
• Add books (title, author, year, ISBN, read/unread status).
• Remove books by ISBN.
• Search books by title or author.
• List all books, sorted by title or year.
• Mark a book as read/unread.
• Statistics: total books, percentage read.
• Persist data to a JSON file.
• User‑friendly menu.
Step 1: Define the Data Model (Book class)

# models.py
import json
from datetime import datetime

class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, year, isbn, read=False):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.year = year
        self.isbn = isbn
        self.read = read
        self.added_date = datetime.now().isoformat()

    def to_dict(self):
        """Convert Book to dictionary for JSON serialization."""
        return {
            "title": self.title,
            "author": self.author,
            "year": self.year,
            "isbn": self.isbn,
            "read": self.read,
            "added_date": self.added_date
        }

    @classmethod
    def from_dict(cls, data):
        """Create Book from dictionary."""
        book = cls(data["title"], data["author"], data["year"], data["isbn"], data["read"])
        book.added_date = data.get("added_date", datetime.now().isoformat())
        return book

    def __str__(self):
        status = "✓ Read" if self.read else "○ Unread"
        return f"{self.title} by {self.author} ({self.year}) [{self.isbn}] {status}"
Step 2: Storage Layer (LibraryStorage)

# storage.py
import json
from pathlib import Path
from models import Book

class LibraryStorage:
    def __init__(self, filename="library.json"):
        self.filename = Path(filename)

    def load(self):
        """Load books from JSON file. Returns list of Book objects."""
        if not self.filename.exists():
            return []
        with open(self.filename, "r") as f:
            data = json.load(f)
        return [Book.from_dict(item) for item in data]

    def save(self, books):
        """Save list of Book objects to JSON file."""
        data = [book.to_dict() for book in books]
        with open(self.filename, "w") as f:
            json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
Step 3: Business Logic (LibraryManager)

# manager.py
from models import Book
from storage import LibraryStorage

class LibraryManager:
    def __init__(self, storage):
        self.storage = storage
        self.books = storage.load()

    def save(self):
        self.storage.save(self.books)

    def add_book(self, title, author, year, isbn, read=False):
        # Validation
        if not title or not author:
            raise ValueError("Title and author are required")
        if self.find_book_by_isbn(isbn):
            raise ValueError(f"Book with ISBN {isbn} already exists")
        book = Book(title, author, year, isbn, read)
        self.books.append(book)
        self.save()
        return book

    def remove_book(self, isbn):
        book = self.find_book_by_isbn(isbn)
        if book:
            self.books.remove(book)
            self.save()
            return True
        return False

    def find_book_by_isbn(self, isbn):
        for book in self.books:
            if book.isbn == isbn:
                return book
        return None

    def search_books(self, query):
        """Search by title or author (case‑insensitive partial match)."""
        query_lower = query.lower()
        results = []
        for book in self.books:
            if query_lower in book.title.lower() or query_lower in book.author.lower():
                results.append(book)
        return results

    def get_all_books(self, sort_by="title"):
        if sort_by == "title":
            return sorted(self.books, key=lambda b: b.title.lower())
        elif sort_by == "year":
            return sorted(self.books, key=lambda b: b.year)
        else:
            return self.books

    def mark_read(self, isbn):
        book = self.find_book_by_isbn(isbn)
        if book:
            book.read = True
            self.save()
            return True
        return False

    def get_stats(self):
        total = len(self.books)
        read_count = sum(1 for b in self.books if b.read)
        percent = (read_count / total * 100) if total > 0 else 0
        return {"total": total, "read": read_count, "percent": percent}
Step 4: User Interface (CLI)

# cli.py
import sys
from manager import LibraryManager
from storage import LibraryStorage

def display_menu():
    print("\n" + "=" * 40)
    print("        PERSONAL LIBRARY MANAGER")
    print("=" * 40)
    print("1. Add a book")
    print("2. Remove a book")
    print("3. Search books")
    print("4. List all books")
    print("5. Mark a book as read")
    print("6. View statistics")
    print("7. Exit")
    print("=" * 40)

def add_book_flow(manager):
    print("\n--- Add a New Book ---")
    title = input("Title: ").strip()
    if not title:
        print("Title cannot be empty.")
        return
    author = input("Author: ").strip()
    if not author:
        print("Author cannot be empty.")
        return
    try:
        year = int(input("Year: "))
    except ValueError:
        print("Invalid year.")
        return
    isbn = input("ISBN: ").strip()
    read_input = input("Have you read it? (y/n): ").strip().lower()
    read = read_input == 'y'
    try:
        book = manager.add_book(title, author, year, isbn, read)
        print(f"✅ Added: {book}")
    except ValueError as e:
        print(f"❌ Error: {e}")

def remove_book_flow(manager):
    isbn = input("Enter ISBN of book to remove: ").strip()
    if manager.remove_book(isbn):
        print("✅ Book removed.")
    else:
        print("❌ Book not found.")

def search_flow(manager):
    query = input("Enter search term (title or author): ").strip()
    results = manager.search_books(query)
    if not results:
        print("No matching books.")
    else:
        print(f"\nFound {len(results)} book(s):")
        for book in results:
            print(f"  {book}")

def list_books_flow(manager):
    print("\nSort by: 1. Title  2. Year")
    choice = input("Choice: ").strip()
    sort_by = "title" if choice == "1" else "year"
    books = manager.get_all_books(sort_by)
    if not books:
        print("Your library is empty.")
    else:
        for book in books:
            print(f"  {book}")

def mark_read_flow(manager):
    isbn = input("Enter ISBN of book to mark as read: ").strip()
    if manager.mark_read(isbn):
        print("✅ Book marked as read.")
    else:
        print("❌ Book not found.")

def stats_flow(manager):
    stats = manager.get_stats()
    print(f"\n📊 Statistics:")
    print(f"  Total books: {stats['total']}")
    print(f"  Books read: {stats['read']}")
    print(f"  Percentage read: {stats['percent']:.1f}%")

def main():
    storage = LibraryStorage("library.json")
    manager = LibraryManager(storage)

    while True:
        display_menu()
        choice = input("Enter your choice: ").strip()
        if choice == "1":
            add_book_flow(manager)
        elif choice == "2":
            remove_book_flow(manager)
        elif choice == "3":
            search_flow(manager)
        elif choice == "4":
            list_books_flow(manager)
        elif choice == "5":
            mark_read_flow(manager)
        elif choice == "6":
            stats_flow(manager)
        elif choice == "7":
            print("Goodbye!")
            break
        else:
            print("Invalid choice. Please try again.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Step 5: Packaging the Application
Create a setup.py or pyproject.toml to make your project installable.

# setup.py (minimal)
from setuptools import setup, find_packages

setup(
    name="library_manager",
    version="1.0.0",
    packages=find_packages(),
    entry_points={
        "console_scripts": [
            "libman = cli:main",
        ]
    },
    author="Your Name",
    description="A personal library manager",
    python_requires=">=3.8",
)
Then install in development mode: pip install -e .. After that, you can run libman from anywhere.
Step 6: Extending the Project (Bonus Ideas)
<ul>
• Add a graphical interface with Tkinter or a web interface with Flask.
• Fetch book details from OpenLibrary API using requests.
• Add a rating system (1‑5 stars).
• Generate reading reports (PDF or HTML).
• Add user accounts with multiple libraries.
Key Takeaways – You Are Now a Python Programmer
<ul>
• You have built a complete, functional application from scratch.
• You applied OOP, file I/O, JSON, error handling, and modular design.
• You learned how to package and distribute a Python application.
• The same principles apply to larger projects – break them into models, storage, business logic, and UI.
• Continue learning: explore databases (SQLite), web frameworks (Flask/Django), data science (pandas), or automation.
• Congratulations on completing the Python Beginner to Intermediate course!

courses.title: Java Programming: Enterprise Object-Oriented Principles
courses.description: Java operates the backbone of enterprise applications natively through the JVM. Learn strict, robust typing schemas, heavy Object-Oriented enforcement, and highly scalable memory garbage-collection mechanics.

modules.title: Module 1: The JVM & Execution Workflow
content: Java is both compiled and interpreted. Your source code (`.java`) is compiled into platform‑independent **bytecode** (`.class`). This bytecode runs on the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** – a virtual computer that translates bytecode into machine code at runtime. This design gives Java its famous motto: 'Write Once, Run Anywhere'.
The Java Execution Pipeline
Hello.java  →  javac (compiler)  →  Hello.class (bytecode)  →  JVM  →  Runs on any OS
<ul>
• Compilation: The Java compiler (javac) checks syntax and generates bytecode – a set of instructions understood by the JVM.
• Class Loading: The JVM loads `.class` files into memory using the ClassLoader.
• Bytecode Verification: The bytecode verifier checks for illegal code (e.g., stack overflows, illegal casts) to ensure security.
• Interpretation & JIT Compilation: The JVM interprets bytecode initially. Frequently executed parts are compiled to native machine code on the fly by the Just‑In‑Time (JIT) compiler for performance.
• Execution: The native code runs on the actual CPU.
Your First Java Program

// Hello.java
public class Hello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, World!");
    }
}
Steps to run:

javac Hello.java      # produces Hello.class
java Hello            # runs the program
The `main` Method – Entry Point
<ul>
• public – accessible from anywhere (JVM needs access).
• static – called without creating an object (JVM invokes it before any objects exist).
• void – returns nothing.
• String[] args – command‑line arguments.
JVM Memory Areas (Simplified)
<ul>
• Stack – stores local variables, method calls (each thread has its own stack).
• Heap – stores all objects (instances of classes) and arrays. Shared across threads.
• Method Area – stores class metadata, static variables, constants, bytecode.
• Program Counter (PC) Register – points to the current instruction being executed.
Garbage Collection (GC)

Java automatically reclaims memory of objects no longer reachable. You don't need to free memory manually (no `free` or `delete`). The JVM runs a garbage collector in the background, which may pause your program briefly. This prevents memory leaks but makes timing less predictable.
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Java source → bytecode → JVM → native execution.
• JVM provides platform independence and security.
• Every Java application starts with public static void main(String[] args).
• JVM manages memory automatically via garbage collection.

modules.title: Module 2: Strict Typing & Primitives
content: Java is a **statically typed** language – every variable must be declared with a type, and that type cannot change. The compiler checks type compatibility before the program runs, catching many errors early. Java has eight **primitive data types** that hold raw values (not objects).
Why Strict Typing?
<ul>
• Detects type errors at compile time (e.g., assigning a String to an int).
• Makes code self‑documenting and predictable.
• Allows the JVM to optimize memory layout and performance.
The 8 Primitive Types

// Integers
byte b = 100;          // 8 bits, -128 to 127
short s = 32000;       // 16 bits
int i = 2_000_000_000; // 32 bits (most common)
long l = 9_000_000_000L; // 64 bits, suffix L

// Floating point
float f = 3.14f;       // 32 bits, suffix f
double d = 3.14159;    // 64 bits (default for decimals)

// Character
char c = 'A';          // 16 bits Unicode (0-65535)

// Boolean
boolean flag = true;   // true or false (not 0/1)
Type Conversion (Casting)
<ul>
• Widening (implicit) – smaller to larger type: int → long → float → double – safe, automatic.
• Narrowing (explicit) – larger to smaller: requires cast (type)value, may lose data.

int x = 100;
long y = x;           // widening – automatic
double z = y;         // widening

long big = 1_000_000_000_000L;
int small = (int) big; // narrowing – data loss (silent!)

// Casting between float and int
float pi = 3.14f;
int piInt = (int) pi;   // 3 (truncates)
Wrapper Classes
Primitives are not objects, but Java provides wrapper classes to 'box' them when needed (e.g., in collections).

Integer iObj = Integer.valueOf(42);   // boxing (explicit)
int i = iObj;                         // unboxing (automatic since Java 5)

// Autoboxing – automatic conversion
Integer auto = 100;   // primitive int → Integer object
int again = auto;      // Integer → int
Variables and Constants

int count = 10;                 // mutable variable
final double PI = 3.14159;      // constant – cannot reassign

// Type inference with 'var' (Java 10+)
var name = "Alice";    // inferred as String
var number = 42;       // inferred as int
Default Values (for class fields)
Local variables must be initialized before use; but instance/static variables get defaults: 0 for numbers, false for boolean, '\u0000' for char, null for objects.
Common Pitfalls
<ul>
• Using == with wrapper objects compares references, not values. Use .equals().
• Floating‑point precision – never compare floats with ==.
• Overflow – int addition can overflow silently; use long or BigInteger for large numbers.
Complete Example

public class PrimitiveDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 10;
        int b = 3;
        double result = (double) a / b;   // explicit cast to avoid integer division
        System.out.printf("%.2f%n", result);  // 3.33

        char grade = 'A';
        int gradeValue = grade;   // implicit widening to int (ASCII/Unicode)
        System.out.println("ASCII of A: " + gradeValue);  // 65

        boolean isValid = true;
        if (isValid) {
            System.out.println("Valid");
        }
    }
}
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Java is statically typed – each variable has a fixed type.
• Eight primitives: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean.
• Widening conversions are automatic; narrowing requires explicit cast.
• Use final for constants.
• Wrapper classes (Integer, Double, etc.) let primitives act as objects.

modules.title: Module 3: Control Flow & Operational Logic
content: Control flow statements decide the order in which code executes. Java provides conditional branching (if, switch), loops (for, while, do-while), and jump statements (break, continue, return). Understanding these is essential for writing dynamic programs.
Conditional Statements
if, else if, else

int score = 85;
if (score >= 90) {
    System.out.println("A");
} else if (score >= 80) {
    System.out.println("B");
} else {
    System.out.println("C or below");
}
switch statement (enhanced in Java 14+)

char grade = 'B';
switch (grade) {
    case 'A' -> System.out.println("Excellent");
    case 'B' -> System.out.println("Good");
    case 'C' -> System.out.println("Fair");
    default -> System.out.println("Needs improvement");
}

// Old style with fall‑through (needs break)
switch (grade) {
    case 'A':
    case 'B':
        System.out.println("Pass");
        break;
    default:
        System.out.println("Fail");
}
Loops
for loop (traditional)

for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    System.out.print(i + " ");   // 0 1 2 3 4
}
Enhanced for‑each loop

int[] numbers = {10, 20, 30};
for (int num : numbers) {
    System.out.println(num);
}
while and do‑while

int count = 0;
while (count < 3) {
    System.out.println(count);
    count++;
}

// do‑while executes at least once
do {
    System.out.println("Runs once");
} while (false);
Jump Statements
<ul>
• break – exits the innermost loop or switch.
• continue – skips the rest of the current iteration.
• return – exits the current method.
• Labeled break/continue – exits outer loops (rare, but useful).

outer: for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
        if (i == 1 && j == 1) break outer;
        System.out.println(i + "," + j);
    }
}
// Prints: 0,0 0,1 0,2 1,0  (stops completely)
Logical Operators
<ul>
• && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT) – short‑circuiting (right side not evaluated if left decides).
• &, | – non‑short‑circuit (always evaluate both sides).

boolean a = true, b = false;
if (a && b) { }   // b not evaluated because a is false
if (a | b) { }    // both evaluated
Ternary Operator
int max = (a > b) ? a : b;
Complete Example – Number Guessing Game

import java.util.Scanner;

public class GuessingGame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        int secret = 42;
        int guess;
        int attempts = 0;

        do {
            System.out.print("Guess the number: ");
            guess = scanner.nextInt();
            attempts++;
            if (guess < secret) {
                System.out.println("Too low!");
            } else if (guess > secret) {
                System.out.println("Too high!");
            }
        } while (guess != secret);

        System.out.println("Correct! Attempts: " + attempts);
        scanner.close();
    }
}
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• if/else for branching; switch for multiple discrete values.
• for when iteration count known; while when condition‑controlled.
• break and continue control loop flow; labeled versions exit outer loops.
• Logical operators short‑circuit; use && and || for efficiency and safety.

modules.title: Module 4: Primitive Arrays & Iterables
content: An array is a fixed‑size, ordered collection of elements of the same type. Arrays are objects in Java (they inherit from Object), but they have special language support. Understanding arrays is fundamental before moving to flexible collections like ArrayList.
Declaring and Creating Arrays

// Declaration
int[] numbers;          // preferred style
int numbers2[];         // C‑style, works but not idiomatic

// Creation with new
numbers = new int[5];   // all elements initialized to 0

// Declaration + initialization
int[] primes = {2, 3, 5, 7, 11};

// Anonymous array
int[] squares = new int[]{1, 4, 9, 16};
Accessing and Modifying

primes[0] = 13;         // change first element
System.out.println(primes[2]); // prints 5
int last = primes[primes.length - 1]; // length is a property
⚠️ Bounds checking: Accessing primes[5] throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime.
Iterating Over Arrays

// Traditional for loop
for (int i = 0; i < primes.length; i++) {
    System.out.println(primes[i]);
}

// Enhanced for‑each
for (int p : primes) {
    System.out.println(p);
}

// Using Arrays utility (java.util.Arrays)
import java.util.Arrays;
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(primes));   // [2,3,5,7,11]
Arrays.sort(primes);                           // in‑place sort
int index = Arrays.binarySearch(primes, 5);    // requires sorted
Multi‑Dimensional Arrays

// 2D array (matrix)
int[][] matrix = new int[3][4];   // 3 rows, 4 columns

// Initializer
int[][] identity = {
    {1, 0},
    {0, 1}
};

// Ragged array (each row can have different length)
int[][] ragged = new int[3][];
ragged[0] = new int[2];
ragged[1] = new int[4];
ragged[2] = new int[1];

// Access
matrix[1][2] = 42;

// Iteration
for (int i = 0; i < matrix.length; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < matrix[i].length; j++) {
        System.out.print(matrix[i][j] + " ");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
Array of Objects

String[] names = new String[3];   // each element is null initially
names[0] = "Alice";
names[1] = "Bob";
// names[2] remains null
The Arrays Utility Class

int[] a = {1,2,3};
int[] b = {1,2,3};
System.out.println(a.equals(b));      // false (different objects)
System.out.println(Arrays.equals(a, b)); // true (contents)

int[] copy = Arrays.copyOf(a, 5);      // {1,2,3,0,0}
int[] range = Arrays.copyOfRange(a, 1, 3); // {2,3}
Arrays.fill(copy, 99);                 // fill entire array with 99
Limitations of Arrays
<ul>
• Fixed size – cannot grow or shrink after creation.
• No built‑in methods for adding/removing elements.
• Only store one type (primitive or object).
For dynamic size, use ArrayList (covered in Module 9).
Complete Example – Grade Statistics

import java.util.Arrays;

public class GradeAnalyzer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] grades = {78, 92, 85, 67, 90, 88};

        // Sum and average
        int sum = 0;
        for (int g : grades) sum += g;
        double avg = (double) sum / grades.length;

        // Sort and find median
        Arrays.sort(grades);
        double median;
        if (grades.length % 2 == 0) {
            median = (grades[grades.length/2 - 1] + grades[grades.length/2]) / 2.0;
        } else {
            median = grades[grades.length/2];
        }

        System.out.println("Average: " + avg);
        System.out.println("Median: " + median);
        System.out.println("Sorted: " + Arrays.toString(grades));
    }
}
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Arrays are fixed‑size, zero‑indexed, and hold elements of a single type.
• Use array.length (not a method).
• java.util.Arrays provides sorting, searching, filling, and copying utilities.
• Multidimensional arrays are arrays of arrays (can be ragged).
• For dynamic resizing, prefer ArrayList from the Collections Framework.

modules.title: Module 5: Core OOP: Classes & Instantiation
content: Java is an object‑oriented language. A class is a blueprint for creating objects. An object is an instance of a class, with its own state (fields) and behavior (methods). This module covers the basics of defining classes, creating objects, constructors, and the this reference.
Defining a Class

public class Student {
    // Fields (instance variables)
    private String name;
    private int id;
    private double gpa;

    // Constructor
    public Student(String name, int id, double gpa) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
        this.gpa = gpa;
    }

    // Method
    public void display() {
        System.out.println(name + " (ID: " + id + "), GPA: " + gpa);
    }

    // Another method
    public void updateGPA(double newGPA) {
        if (newGPA >= 0.0 && newGPA <= 4.0) {
            this.gpa = newGPA;
        }
    }
}
Creating Objects (Instantiation)

Student s1 = new Student("Alice", 101, 3.8);
Student s2 = new Student("Bob", 102, 3.5);

s1.display();
s2.updateGPA(3.9);
Constructors
<ul>
• Have the same name as the class.
• No return type (not even void).
• If you don't provide any constructor, Java adds a default no‑arg constructor.
• You can have multiple constructors (overloading).

public class Point {
    int x, y;
    // No‑arg constructor
    public Point() {
        this(0, 0);   // calls the other constructor
    }
    // Parameterized constructor
    public Point(int x, int y) {
        this.x = x;
        this.y = y;
    }
}
The `this` Keyword
<ul>
• Refers to the current object instance.
• Used to distinguish fields from parameters (common in constructors).
• Can be used to call another constructor (this(...)) – must be the first statement.
Static Members
Static fields and methods belong to the class, not to any instance. They are shared across all objects.

public class Counter {
    private static int count = 0;   // shared counter

    public Counter() {
        count++;
    }

    public static int getCount() {
        return count;
    }
}

Counter c1 = new Counter();
Counter c2 = new Counter();
System.out.println(Counter.getCount());   // 2
Instance vs. Static – Memory Perspective
<ul>
• Instance fields: each object has its own copy.
• Static fields: one copy stored in the method area (class metadata).
• Static methods cannot access instance fields directly (no this).
Method Overloading
Multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists (type or count). Return type alone is not sufficient.

public class MathUtils {
    public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    public double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
    public int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
}
Complete Example – Bank Account

public class BankAccount {
    private String owner;
    private double balance;
    private static int totalAccounts = 0;

    public BankAccount(String owner, double initialDeposit) {
        this.owner = owner;
        this.balance = initialDeposit;
        totalAccounts++;
    }

    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }

    public boolean withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
            balance -= amount;
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.printf("%s: $%.2f%n", owner, balance);
    }

    public static int getTotalAccounts() {
        return totalAccounts;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc1 = new BankAccount("Alice", 1000);
        BankAccount acc2 = new BankAccount("Bob", 500);
        acc1.deposit(200);
        acc2.withdraw(100);
        acc1.display();
        acc2.display();
        System.out.println("Total accounts: " + BankAccount.getTotalAccounts());
    }
}
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• A class is a blueprint; an object is an instance.
• Constructors initialize new objects; use this to refer to current instance.
• Static members belong to the class, not instances.
• Method overloading provides multiple versions of a method with different parameters.

modules.title: Module 6: Encapsulation & Access Modifiers
content: Encapsulation is the principle of hiding internal details and protecting data from unauthorized access. In Java, you achieve encapsulation using access modifiers (private, protected, public, package‑private) and providing controlled access via getters and setters. This leads to more maintainable and secure code.
Access Modifiers – Who Can See What?
<ul>
• private – accessible only within the same class.
• (default / package‑private) – accessible within the same package (no keyword).
• protected – accessible within same package + subclasses (even in different packages).
• public – accessible from anywhere.

package school;

public class Person {
    private String ssn;          // only Person class
    String name;                 // package‑private (within school package)
    protected int age;           // same package + subclasses
    public String country;       // everywhere
}
Why Encapsulation?
<ul>
• Protects integrity – prevent invalid states (e.g., negative age).
• Hides implementation – you can change internal logic without affecting clients.
• Promotes loose coupling – objects interact through well‑defined interfaces.
Getters and Setters

public class BankAccount {
    private double balance;

    // Getter
    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }

    // Setter with validation
    public void setBalance(double balance) {
        if (balance >= 0) {
            this.balance = balance;
        } else {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Balance cannot be negative");
        }
    }
}

// Usage
BankAccount acc = new BankAccount();
acc.setBalance(1000);
System.out.println(acc.getBalance());
Immutable Classes (No Setters)
An immutable object cannot be changed after creation. Useful for thread‑safe, predictable objects (like String).

public final class Point {
    private final int x;
    private final int y;

    public Point(int x, int y) {
        this.x = x;
        this.y = y;
    }

    public int getX() { return x; }
    public int getY() { return y; }
    // No setter methods
}
The `static` and Encapsulation
Static fields are also encapsulated. Use private static fields with public static getters/setters if needed.

public class Configuration {
    private static String apiKey = "default";

    public static String getApiKey() {
        return apiKey;
    }

    public static void setApiKey(String key) {
        if (key != null && !key.isEmpty()) {
            apiKey = key;
        }
    }
}
Records (Java 14+) – Automatic Encapsulation
Records are transparent carriers for immutable data. They generate constructor, accessors, equals(), hashCode(), and toString().

public record Student(String name, int id, double gpa) {}

Student s = new Student("Alice", 101, 3.8);
System.out.println(s.name());   // accessor method
// s.id() returns 101, etc. No setters – immutable
Complete Example – Employee Management

public class Employee {
    private String name;
    private double salary;
    private static int employeeCount = 0;

    public Employee(String name, double salary) {
        setName(name);
        setSalary(salary);
        employeeCount++;
    }

    public String getName() { return name; }
    public void setName(String name) {
        if (name == null || name.trim().isEmpty()) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Name cannot be empty");
        }
        this.name = name;
    }

    public double getSalary() { return salary; }
    public void setSalary(double salary) {
        if (salary < 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Salary cannot be negative");
        }
        this.salary = salary;
    }

    public static int getEmployeeCount() { return employeeCount; }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return String.format("Employee{name='%s', salary=%.2f}", name, salary);
    }
}

// Usage
Employee e = new Employee("Alice", 50000);
e.setSalary(55000);
System.out.println(e);
System.out.println("Total employees: " + Employee.getEmployeeCount());
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Use private for fields by default; expose only what is necessary.
• Getters and setters allow controlled access and validation.
• Immutable classes (no setters, final fields) are safer for sharing.
• Records provide a concise way to create immutable data carriers.
• Encapsulation is a cornerstone of OOP – keep data and behavior together, hide internals.

modules.title: Module 7: Inheritance & Deep Polymorphism
content: Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties and methods from another class (parent/superclass). Polymorphism lets you treat objects of different subclasses uniformly through a common supertype. These are fundamental for code reuse and designing extensible systems.
Basic Inheritance – `extends`

public class Animal {
    protected String name;

    public Animal(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    public void speak() {
        System.out.println(name + " makes a sound.");
    }
}

public class Dog extends Animal {
    public Dog(String name) {
        super(name);   // call parent constructor
    }

    @Override
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println(name + " barks.");
    }

    public void wagTail() {
        System.out.println(name + " wags tail.");
    }
}
The `super` Keyword
<ul>
• Calls parent constructor (super(...)) – must be first statement in child constructor.
• Access parent methods/fields (super.method()).
Method Overriding
A subclass can provide a specific implementation of a method defined in the parent. Use @Override annotation (optional but recommended).
<ul>
• Same method name, return type (or covariant), parameters.
• Access level cannot be more restrictive (e.g., cannot override a public method with private).
Polymorphism – The Real Power

Animal a1 = new Animal("Generic");
Animal a2 = new Dog("Buddy");   // Dog is an Animal

a1.speak();   // Generic makes a sound.
a2.speak();   // Buddy barks.  (polymorphic call)

// a2.wagTail(); // Compile error – reference type Animal doesn't know wagTail()
Upcasting and Downcasting
<ul>
• Upcasting (implicit): Animal a = new Dog(); – safe, always works.
• Downcasting (explicit): Dog d = (Dog) a; – may fail with ClassCastException. Use instanceof to check.

if (a2 instanceof Dog) {
    Dog d = (Dog) a2;
    d.wagTail();
}
Abstract Classes and Methods
An abstract class cannot be instantiated; it may contain abstract methods (no body) that subclasses must implement.

public abstract class Shape {
    protected String color;

    public Shape(String color) { this.color = color; }

    public abstract double area();    // no body

    public String getColor() { return color; }
}

public class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(String color, double radius) {
        super(color);
        this.radius = radius;
    }

    @Override
    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}
Interfaces – Pure Contracts
An interface defines a set of method signatures (and constants). A class can implement multiple interfaces.

public interface Drawable {
    void draw();
}

public interface Resizable {
    void resize(double factor);
}

public class Circle extends Shape implements Drawable, Resizable {
    // must implement draw() and resize()
    @Override
    public void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing circle"); }

    @Override
    public void resize(double factor) { radius *= factor; }
}
Default and Static Methods in Interfaces (Java 8+)
Interfaces can have default implementations (default) and static methods.

public interface Logger {
    void log(String message);

    default void logError(String error) {
        log("ERROR: " + error);
    }

    static void info(String msg) {
        System.out.println("INFO: " + msg);
    }
}
Complete Example – Polymorphic Payment System

interface Payable {
    double calculatePayment();
}

abstract class Employee implements Payable {
    protected String name;
    public Employee(String name) { this.name = name; }
}

class SalariedEmployee extends Employee {
    private double annualSalary;

    public SalariedEmployee(String name, double salary) {
        super(name);
        this.annualSalary = salary;
    }

    @Override
    public double calculatePayment() {
        return annualSalary / 12;   // monthly
    }
}

class HourlyEmployee extends Employee {
    private double hourlyRate;
    private int hoursWorked;

    public HourlyEmployee(String name, double rate, int hours) {
        super(name);
        this.hourlyRate = rate;
        this.hoursWorked = hours;
    }

    @Override
    public double calculatePayment() {
        return hourlyRate * hoursWorked;
    }
}

public class Payroll {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Payable[] employees = {
            new SalariedEmployee("Alice", 60000),
            new HourlyEmployee("Bob", 20, 160)
        };

        for (Payable p : employees) {
            System.out.printf("Payment: $%.2f%n", p.calculatePayment());
        }
    }
}
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Inheritance (extends) creates an is‑a relationship; use super to access parent.
• Method overriding enables dynamic (runtime) polymorphism.
• Abstract classes provide partial implementation; interfaces define contracts (multiple inheritance of type).
• Polymorphism allows writing generic code that works on supertypes.
• Prefer composition over inheritance when possible, but inheritance is powerful for true is‑a hierarchies.

modules.title: Module 8: String Manipulation & Memory Constraints
content: Strings are everywhere in Java. The String class is immutable – once created, its value cannot change. This has important implications for performance and memory. Java also provides StringBuilder and StringBuffer for efficient mutable string operations. This module explores string handling, interning, and memory considerations.
String Immutability

String s = "Hello";
s = s + " World";   // creates a NEW string, "Hello" is lost (garbage)
Why immutable?
<ul>
• Thread‑safe – no synchronization needed.
• Can be interned (shared) to save memory.
• Hashcode is cached – efficient for hash maps.
String Pool (Interning)
String literals are stored in a special heap area called the String Pool. If you create the same literal twice, Java reuses the same object.

String a = "Hello";
String b = "Hello";
System.out.println(a == b);   // true (same object)

String c = new String("Hello");   // forces new object (not interned)
System.out.println(a == c);   // false (different objects)
System.out.println(a.equals(c));   // true (same content)
You can manually intern: c.intern().
Common String Methods

String s = "  Hello, World!  ";
s = s.trim();                      // "Hello, World!"
System.out.println(s.length());    // 13
System.out.println(s.charAt(7));   // 'W'
System.out.println(s.substring(7, 12)); // "World"
System.out.println(s.toLowerCase());
System.out.println(s.replace('l', 'p')); // "Heppo, Worpd!"
String[] parts = s.split(", ");   // ["Hello", "World!"]
boolean contains = s.contains("World"); // true
int index = s.indexOf("o");        // 4
boolean matches = s.matches("Hello.*"); // true
StringBuilder and StringBuffer – Mutable Strings
For repeated modifications (e.g., in loops), use StringBuilder (not thread‑safe, faster) or StringBuffer (thread‑safe, slower).

StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
