• Token Usage: Per request and aggregated.
• Latency Percentiles: p50, p95, p99.
• Error Rate: API failures, timeouts, validation errors.
• Output Quality: LLM-as-judge scores over time (drift detection).

# Using OpenTelemetry to trace LLM calls
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_call") as span:
    span.set_attribute("prompt", prompt)
    response = call_llm(prompt)
    span.set_attribute("response", response)
    span.set_attribute("tokens_used", response.usage.total_tokens)
Key Takeaway
Treat LLM calls like any other production service: instrument, monitor, alert.

modules.title: Module 10: Capstone - Architecting a Multi-Agent LLM System
content: Design and implement a complete, production-ready multi-agent system to solve a complex real-world problem.
Project: Autonomous Customer Support Orchestrator
Build a system that:
<ul>
• Receives a customer support ticket.
• Uses a Router Agent to classify intent (Billing, Technical, Account).
• Dispatches to a Specialist Agent (e.g., Billing Agent) with access to relevant tools (lookup invoice, issue refund).
• A Critic Agent reviews the proposed response for tone and policy compliance.
• If confidence is low, escalates to a human with a summary.
Architecture Diagram
[User] -> [Router Agent] -> [Specialist Agent] -> [Critic Agent] -> [Human Escalation (optional)] -> [User]
Implementation Skeleton

class SupportOrchestrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.router = RouterAgent()
        self.specialists = {
            "billing": BillingAgent(),
            "tech": TechSupportAgent(),
            "account": AccountAgent()
        }
        self.critic = CriticAgent()
        self.human_escalation_threshold = 0.7
    
    def handle_ticket(self, ticket):
        intent, confidence = self.router.classify(ticket)
        specialist = self.specialists.get(intent)
        if not specialist:
            return self.escalate_to_human(ticket, "Unknown intent")
        
        draft_response = specialist.generate_response(ticket)
        review = self.critic.review(draft_response, ticket)
        
        if review['score'] < self.human_escalation_threshold:
            return self.escalate_to_human(ticket, review['feedback'])
        
        return draft_response
Congratulations!
You have achieved professional mastery in prompt engineering and LLM system design. You are now equipped to architect AI systems that are robust, scalable, and reliable.

courses.title: Production SaaS DevOps Engineering: Multi‑Tenant Real‑Time Systems
courses.description: Learn to design, deploy, and operate production‑grade, multi‑tenant, real‑time SaaS platforms. This course interweaves system architecture, secure CI/CD enforcement, Kubernetes on GKE Autopilot, WebSocket scaling with Redis, database operations (Supabase), secrets management, security pipelines, observability, cost engineering, incident response, Infrastructure as Code, and audit‑ready engineering. You will build the discipline to deliver secure, cost‑optimised, and compliant systems.

modules.title: Module 1 — System Architecture -> Foundation
content: A production SaaS system is not a monolith; it is a fabric of loosely coupled, independently scalable services that must preserve tenant data isolation while delivering real‑time experiences. This module establishes the foundational architecture patterns that separate a prototype from a production‑grade multi‑tenant application. You will learn to think in terms of failure domains, data‑flow mapping, and consistency‑versus‑latency trade‑offs, enabling you to design systems that survive component failures and traffic spikes gracefully.
Multi‑Tenant SaaS Architecture – Tenant Isolation Strategies

Multi‑tenancy means a single instance of software serves multiple customers (tenants). The primary challenge is isolating tenant data and performance. There are three classic strategies, each with different trade‑offs in security, cost, and operational complexity:
<ul>

• Database‑per‑tenant: Each tenant gets a dedicated database. This provides the strongest isolation—data is physically separated, making backup/restore per tenant trivial and reducing the blast radius of a security breach. However, it has the highest operational overhead because each database requires maintenance (upgrades, scaling) and cost rises linearly with tenant count.

• Schema‑per‑tenant: Tenants share a single database server but each has a separate schema (namespace). Isolation is strong at the logical level, and management is easier than separate databases because you only maintain one server. Migrations, however, must be applied to every schema, which can become a complex orchestration task as the number of tenants grows.

• Discriminator column (shared tables): A tenant_id column exists in every table, and every query must filter on it. This is the cheapest to operate and the simplest to manage, but isolation depends entirely on the application layer—a single missing WHERE clause leaks data across tenants. Modern practices like Row‑Level Security (RLS) (see Module 5) can enforce this at the database level, making the shared‑table approach secure enough for many SaaS applications.

Practical Implementation: Regardless of the strategy chosen, tenant context must be propagated through every API call, background job, and WebSocket connection. A gateway or middleware must extract tenant identity (e.g., from a JWT claim, API key, or x-tenant-id header) and inject it into the request context. For example, in a Node.js Express middleware:

function tenantResolver(req, res, next) {
  const tenantId = req.headers['x-tenant-id'] || req.user?.tenant_id;
  if (!tenantId) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Tenant ID required' });
  req.tenant_id = tenantId;
  next();
}

In a background worker, the tenant ID must be part of the job payload and used to scope database queries. The architecture must treat tenant context as non‑negotiable, threading it through every layer.
Real‑Time System Design – Event‑Driven vs Request‑Response

A request‑response model (client polls the server) works for low‑frequency updates and is simple to scale horizontally behind a load balancer. However, for chat, collaborative editing, or live dashboards, it introduces latency and wasted resources. An event‑driven architecture, using persistent connections (WebSockets) and a publish‑subscribe broker (Redis), pushes updates to clients the moment data changes.
Trade‑offs in depth:
<ul>

• Request‑response (polling): Stateless, easy to cache, works with any CDN. But it creates a polling overhead—even if no data has changed, the server still receives requests. Long polling (holding the request open until data is available) reduces this but still ties up server threads and makes load balancing less predictable.

• Event‑driven (WebSocket): Stateful, real‑time delivery, but introduces connection management complexity. The server must track open sockets, handle reconnections, and scale the pub/sub backplane. It also requires a WebSocket‑aware load balancer or an ingress that supports protocol upgrade.

A production system rarely uses one exclusively. The best pattern is hybrid: REST for CRUD mutations (POST /messages), WebSocket for subscriptions (client subscribes to a topic after connection), and a server‑sent events (SSE) alternative for simpler one‑way streaming. The decision is based on user experience: if millisecond‑level latency is required, WebSocket is mandatory; if seconds work, polling suffices and reduces operational burden.
Separation of Concerns – API Service, WebSocket Service, Background Workers
A production SaaS decomposes into at least three service roles, each with distinct scalability profiles and failure domains:
<ul>

• API Service: Stateless HTTP handlers for CRUD operations, authentication, and admin tasks. Horizontally scales with a load balancer. It handles short‑lived requests and returns JSON. Because it is stateless, you can safely terminate any pod at any time.

• WebSocket Service: Stateful process that maintains long‑lived connections to clients. Usually requires sticky sessions (cookies) or a Redis pub/sub backplane to share events across instances. It manages connection lifecycle (authentication, heartbeats, reconnection), enforces message rate limits, and pushes real‑time data. Cannot be killed abruptly without affecting clients (see graceful shutdown in Module 4).

• Background Workers: Processes that consume work queues (e.g., sending emails, generating thumbnails, processing webhooks). They are stateless from the queue’s perspective—they pull jobs from a reliable queue (Redis, Cloud Tasks) and execute them. Workers scale independently based on queue depth; they are easy to replace and restart.

Practical architecture: The API service does not directly talk to WebSocket pods; instead, it publishes events to Redis after persisting data. WebSocket pods subscribe to relevant channels and push to clients. Workers may also publish events (e.g., “thumbnail ready”) through the same Redis mechanism. This decouples all three roles, allowing independent development, deployment, and scaling.
Stateful vs Stateless Services
Understanding the distinction is critical for scaling and resilience:
<ul>

• Stateless services (REST APIs, workers) do not hold any session‑specific data in memory between requests. You can add more replicas, kill any one, and the load balancer will redirect traffic without disruption. They are “cattle”, not “pets”.

• Stateful services (WebSocket servers, databases, Redis) hold data that cannot be discarded—WebSocket connections, in‑memory caches, or on‑disk databases. To scale a stateful service, you must externalise state to a fast, shared store (Redis) or use consistent hashing to partition connections. Stateful services are harder to deploy because you must drain connections before termination (see Module 4, SIGTERM handling).

Practical rule: Keep the number of stateful services to a minimum. When you must use them, treat them as the “load‑bearing” components with extra operational care: health checks, graceful shutdown, and backup/restore procedures.
Horizontal Scalability Boundaries
Not every component scales horizontally without limit. The main bottlenecks are:
<ul>

• Database writes: In most SQL setups, you have a single primary for writes. Read replicas can scale reads, but write throughput is finite. Sharding or partitioning can extend writes, but they add application complexity.

• WebSocket backplane: If you use Redis pub/sub to fan‑out messages, Redis itself becomes the scalability limit. A single Redis instance can handle millions of messages per second, but beyond that you need clustering. Monitor Redis CPU and memory.

• Load balancers / Ingress: These are highly scalable, but downstream services must handle the fan‑out. If the WebSocket service cannot keep up, the load balancer may buffer connections, causing backpressure.

Capacity planning: Map the scaling limits of each component and design your system so that you can add instances up to a known ceiling before needing architectural changes. For example, a single Redis pub/sub node can support ~100k concurrent WebSocket connections with typical message rates; beyond that, use Redis Cluster or consider a dedicated real‑time service like Ably.
Data Flow Mapping – Client → API → Redis → DB → Client
The canonical real‑time data flow in a SaaS application:
<ol>
• Client sends an HTTP POST to the API service (e.g., POST /channels/123/messages).
• API service validates the request, enforces rate limits, and inserts the message into the PostgreSQL database (durable storage).
• Upon successful database commit, the API publishes an event to Redis on a tenant‑scoped channel (e.g., tenant:tenant_id:channel:channel_id).
• All WebSocket server instances subscribed to that channel receive the event.
• Each WebSocket server looks up which locally connected clients belong to that tenant/channel and pushes the message to them.

Failure scenarios: If Redis is down between steps 3 and 4, the message is still in the database but not delivered live. Clients will receive it later via reconciliation (Module 4). If a WebSocket server crashes after step 4, reconnecting clients will fetch the missing message from the database. This design ensures durability (database) and low‑latency delivery (Redis) are separated. It also means that the API acknowledges the HTTP request only after the database write, not after delivery, maintaining consistency.
Failure Domains in Distributed Systems
A failure domain is a component or group of components that can fail together (e.g., an availability zone, a single Redis instance). To design for resilience:
<ul>
• Isolate by service role: Deploy API, WebSocket, and worker pods in different namespaces with separate resource quotas. Network policies should prevent one from overwhelming the other.

• Zone redundancy: Spread pods across multiple availability zones (AZs) so that a single AZ failure does not take down the whole service. GKE Autopilot does this automatically if you configure a multi‑zone cluster.

• Independent data stores: Do not share the same database cluster for wildly different workloads (e.g., chat messages and analytics queries). Separate them so that a heavy analytics query does not lock the chat table.

• Graceful degradation: If the WebSocket service fails, the API should still accept writes (since the database is up) and indicate that real‑time updates are delayed. The system degrades, not crashes.

Practical exercise: Draw a failure tree: for each component, list what happens if it fails (does it cause a full outage? can we serve stale data?). Then implement health checks and circuit breakers accordingly.
Latency‑Sensitive vs Consistency‑Sensitive Paths
Not all operations require the same degree of consistency. Separate them:
<ul>

• Consistency‑sensitive path (writes): When a user sends a chat message, you must commit it to the primary database before acknowledging success. This guarantees the message is never lost. The write operation is therefore bound by database latency.

• Latency‑sensitive path (delivery): Once the message is durable, deliver it to clients as fast as possible. Use Redis pub/sub, which has sub‑millisecond latency. No round‑trip to the database is required.

• Reads: Fetching message history can tolerate a slightly stale view (e.g., from a read replica) if it reduces load on the primary. However, for a user’s own messages immediately after posting, you may need to read from the primary to avoid the “my message disappeared” illusion.

Implementation tip: In the API, after a POST, return the full message object directly from the database insert rather than waiting for a WebSocket echo. This gives the client instant confirmation. The WebSocket delivery then becomes a non‑blocking bonus.
WebSocket vs Polling Trade‑offs
A detailed comparison to guide architectural decisions:
<table style='width:100%; border-collapse: collapse;'>

<tr><th style='border:1px solid #ddd; padding:8px;'>Aspect</th><th style='border:1px solid #ddd; padding:8px;'>WebSocket</th><th style='border:1px solid #ddd; padding:8px;'>Polling (HTTP short/long)</th></tr>
LatencyNear real‑time (ms)Seconds (depends on interval)
Resource usagePersistent TCP connection; memory per socketNew TCP connection per poll; overhead of HTTP headers
Infrastructure requirementsWebSocket‑aware load balancer / ingressStandard HTTP, any CDN works
ComplexityStateful; needs reconnection logic, backpressureStateless; simple, but long polling requires timeout handling
Battery/mobileMaintains radio awake, may drain batteryLong polling can be battery‑friendly if intervals are sparse

Decision heuristic: Use WebSockets when user experience demands real‑time interactivity and you can invest in the operational complexity (load balancer configuration, connection draining). Use polling (or SSE) for dashboards that refresh every 15+ seconds, for mobile apps with intermittent connectivity, or when infrastructure simplicity is paramount.
Control Plane vs Data Plane in SaaS Systems
In a multi‑tenant application, operations can be split into two planes:
<ul>

• Data plane: Handles tenant‑facing, latency‑sensitive traffic: chat messages, file uploads, real‑time edits. This is where user experience is measured and where revenue is generated. It must scale with usage and often has a higher availability requirement.

• Control plane: Handles administrative tasks: tenant provisioning, plan upgrades, user management, billing, and analytics. It is less latency‑sensitive and typically experiences lower traffic. A control plane failure should never disrupt the data plane.

Implementation: Separate these into different services or even different clusters. For example, the control plane may run as a separate deployment with its own database, while the data plane uses independent, high‑performance resources. API routes can be prefixed with /api/v1/ for data plane and /admin/ for control, routed to different backends. This separation also aids security: control plane endpoints may require stronger authentication and audit logging.

Real‑world example: In a SaaS chat product, the data plane handles message sending and receiving, file sharing, and real‑time typing indicators. The control plane handles workspace creation, user invitations, billing changes, and usage reports. If the billing system (control plane) goes down, users can still chat—they just can’t upgrade their plan at that moment.

modules.title: Module 2 — CI/CD Pipeline Engineering
content: CI/CD is not just an automation tool; it is the enforcement system that ensures every code change meets security, quality, and compliance gates before reaching production. This module transforms your view of pipelines from a convenience into a production safety net. You will learn to design immutable artifact pipelines, enforce branch protection, and embed security scans as gating mechanisms that prevent bad code from ever seeing the light of a production environment.
Pipeline as Enforcement System (Not Automation Tool)

Automation is the ability to run tests and builds automatically. Enforcement means that the pipeline’s verdict is final: a failed stage blocks the change from progressing, period. This mindset is crucial for shifting from “we have CI” to “we have a production‑ready system.” An enforcement pipeline acts as a contract between development and operations—if the pipeline passes, the artefact is safe to deploy; if it fails, the change must be reworked. To implement enforcement, you must:
<ul>

• Define clear policies: Every rule that must be satisfied (unit tests pass, linting clean, SAST scan shows no HIGH/CRITICAL findings, dependency audit returns zero critical vulnerabilities) must be encoded as a pipeline stage.

• Use branch protection rules: On GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket, configure branch protection to require specific checks to pass before merging. For example, on GitHub, you can set a branch protection rule on main that requires status checks like build, test, security-scan to pass, along with at least one approval. Even repository administrators must follow this rule.

• Immutable pipeline definitions: Store the CI/CD configuration (e.g., .github/workflows/*.yml) in the same repository, but protect it from casual modification. Changes to the pipeline itself must go through the same PR process, preventing developers from relaxing checks to “get the deploy out”.
• No bypass button: In a true enforcement model, there is no “skip checks” option. Emergency changes use a separate, heavily audited hotfix path that still runs a subset of critical checks.
Practical example – GitHub branch protection:

# GitHub branch protection settings (can be managed via API/Terraform as well)
# Require pull request reviews before merging: 1 approval required
# Require status checks to pass before merging:
#   - build-and-test
#   - semgrep-sast
#   - trivy-image-scan
#   - dependency-audit
# Include administrators: true
# Allow force pushes: false
# Allow deletions: false
The pipeline becomes the single source of truth for a change’s readiness. Every merge to main proves that the code passed all tests and scans, creating an auditable record.
Branch Protection & Release Discipline

Branch protection is the backbone of enforcement. Without it, developers can push directly to production branches, bypassing all quality gates. The discipline extends beyond protection rules to a clearly defined branching strategy:
<ul>
• Main branch (main): Always deployable. Only merged via pull requests that passed the pipeline and received human review. This is the source for releases. Never commit directly.

• Feature branches: Short‑lived branches (prefixed with feature/, fix/, chore/) created off main. Developers work here, push, and open PRs. These branches are tested in the CI pipeline but are never deployed to production.

• Release branches (release/v1.2): Cut from main when preparing a release. Only bug fixes are cherry‑picked here. This allows stabilising a release without blocking new work on main. Merges back to main after release.

• Hotfix branches: Created from main to quickly fix critical production issues. After testing, merged to main and the active release branch (if any). Hotfixes still require pipeline execution but might follow a streamlined path with higher manual approval oversight.

Release discipline: A release is a deliberate act. Do not deploy every merge to main automatically to production unless you have robust canary analysis and feature flags. Instead, use a scheduled release cadence or manual promotion. For example, a CI pipeline on merge to main builds the artifact and deploys to staging. After validation, a separate release pipeline deploys the same artifact to production. This separation ensures that main is always in a deployable state but not automatically live.
Immutable Artifacts – Build Once, Deploy Everywhere

Rebuilding an artefact at each environment (dev → staging → prod) breaks traceability because compilation timestamps, dependencies, and even compiler versions may differ. The result: you test something in staging but deploy a different binary to production. Immutable artifacts solve this by building exactly once, stamping it with a unique version, and then promoting that same artefact through environments.
<ul>

• Container images: Build the Docker image, tag it with the git commit SHA (e.g., myapp:abc123) and also a build ID (myapp:build-456). Push to a secure registry like Google Artifact Registry. The deployment configuration for each environment simply references the same image tag. The image never changes.

• Non‑container artefacts: For JARs, binaries, or static assets, store them in an artefact repository (Nexus, Artifactory, GCS) with the same immutable version. Use checksums to verify integrity at deploy time.

# Building an immutable Docker image with Git SHA tag
export IMAGE_TAG=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/$PROJECT_ID/myapp:$CI_COMMIT_SHA
docker build -t $IMAGE_TAG .
docker push $IMAGE_TAG
# Optionally add a 'latest' tag only for dev environment; never for prod.
Key benefits:
<ul>
• The exact artefact that passed integration tests in staging is guaranteed to be the one in production.
• Rollback is trivial: just revert the deployment to the previous image tag.
• Audit trails are clear: you can trace a running container back to a git commit and build job.
Multi‑Stage Pipelines – Build → Scan → Deploy
A well‑designed CI/CD pipeline is broken into distinct stages, each with a specific purpose and failure mode. A canonical multi‑stage pipeline for a containerised application might look like this:
<ol>
• Source Checkout & Pre‑checks: Lint code, check formatting, run secret detection (pre‑commit style). Fast feedback.
• Build: Compile, package, build Docker image. Also generate SBOM and run static analysis on source (SAST) in parallel if performance allows.
• Unit & Integration Tests: Run against a short‑lived database/Redis instance if needed.
• Security Scans: Image vulnerability scanning (Trivy), dependency audit, containers scan. Blocking stage: if critical/high findings appear, the pipeline stops here.
• Artifact Push: Push image to registry only if all previous stages passed.
• Deploy to Staging: Apply Kubernetes manifests to staging namespace. Run smoke tests (e.g., hitting a health endpoint).
• Integration/Acceptance Tests: Longer‑running tests (DAST with ZAP, end‑to‑end UI tests) can be performed here.
• Manual Approval Gate: A human reviews the staging test results, checks monitoring, and approves.
• Deploy to Production: Deploy to prod, often with a canary or rolling update strategy.

Each stage is a potential gate; a failure at any blocking stage prevents the artefact from moving forward. Stages like DAST might be non‑blocking initially until they mature, but the goal is to make every stage blocking for production deployments.
Environment Promotion Strategy (Dev → Staging → Prod)

Environment promotion is the process of moving an artefact through environments with increasing levels of production‑likeness. Never promote directly from dev to prod; the path must include a staging (or pre‑prod) environment that mirrors production as closely as possible:
<ul>
• Dev: Where developers merge feature branches. Can be a shared namespace with relaxed resources and fast deployment. Often automatically deployed.

• Staging: A dedicated environment that runs the same infrastructure configuration (same database type, same memory requests, same Kubernetes manifests) as production but with synthetic data. This is the final quality gate. Run integration tests, performance checks, and security scans here.
• Canary (optional but recommended): A small subset of production traffic (e.g., 1‑5%) routed to the new version. Allows real‑world validation with quick rollback if errors spike.
• Production: Full rollout after canary succeeds.

Promotion mechanics: The pipeline does not rebuild; it uses the exact image tag from the registry. Deployment manifests for each environment can differ only in parameters (replica count, resource limits, environment variables) injected via Helm or Kustomize. Never use different image tags for different environments.
Example Helm promotion:

# For staging
helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart --set image.tag=$CI_COMMIT_SHA --namespace staging

# For production (after approval)
helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart --set image.tag=$CI_COMMIT_SHA --namespace production
Artifact Versioning & Traceability
Every artefact must be uniquely identifiable and traceable back to its source code commit, build job, and who triggered it. Versioning strategies:
<ul>
• Git commit SHA: Universally unique, immutable, and directly links to the code. Use as the primary tag.
• Build number: A sequential integer or timestamp‑based ID. Provides a human‑friendly version but should be paired with SHA.
• Semantic version: For end‑user releases (v1.2.3). Can be derived from git tags. Automate the tagging with tools like semantic-release.
Embedding metadata: Inject the version, commit SHA, and build timestamp into the application itself so that any running instance can report its identity:

# Go build with ldflags to embed version info
go build -ldflags "-X main.Version=$VERSION -X main.Commit=$COMMIT_SHA -X main.BuildTime=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" -o app .

In a Python app, you might have a /version endpoint that returns a JSON including the git hash. This is invaluable during incident response—you can query the endpoint of a misbehaving instance and immediately know which code it runs.

Traceability to pipeline: Store the pipeline run URL as a label on the image or as a separate annotation in the registry. When you need to review why a build was created, you can follow that link back to the exact CI job. Tools like Google Cloud Build automatically add labels like revision and build-id.
Manual Approval Gates – Why They Matter in Production
Automated gates are fast and consistent, but a human must have the final say before a deployment hits production. Reasons:
<ul>

• Context awareness: A human knows if a marketing campaign is about to start, if another related service is being deployed, or if the on‑call team is dealing with a SEV incident. The pipeline lacks this business context.
• Last‑mile safety net: Despite passing all automated checks, a human can spot anomalies: a DR test running concurrently, a warning in logs that might be unrelated but suspicious.
• Compliance: Many frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001) require change management approval. A manual gate with a timestamped approval record satisfies these requirements.

Implementation: Use pipeline features like GitHub Environments with required reviewers. For example, create a production environment in GitHub and set required reviewers. The pipeline job will wait until an authorised person clicks “Approve”. In GitLab, use when: manual jobs within a stage. The approver must be a member of a specific group, and the approval is logged.

# GitHub Actions: manual approval using environment
jobs:
  deploy-prod:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production  # has required reviewers
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: helm upgrade ...

Manual gates should not be used for staging (where speed matters) but are mandatory for production. The gate should be a decision point, not a rubber‑stamp; the approver should have quick access to staging validation results, dashboards, and recent incident reports.
Pipeline Failure as Safety Mechanism

A red pipeline is not a nuisance—it is the system correctly preventing a potentially harmful change from progressing. Cultivate a culture where a failing security stage is celebrated as a bug caught early. Key principles:
<ul>

• Never greenline: Do not disable a failing stage to rush a deploy. If a rule produces a false positive, update the rule’s exceptions with justification and a time‑limited waiver, and keep the stage running.
• Fail fast: The earlier a pipeline fails, the quicker the feedback. Put fast linting and SAST checks first. The developer should know within minutes if they introduced a vulnerability or breakage.
• Actionable logs: A failed stage must provide a clear error message and a link to a runbook. For example, Trivy should output the CVE list and a remediation command.
• Escalate persistent failures: If a specific stage fails repeatedly across builds, trigger an alert to the platform team so they can investigate the root cause (e.g., a broken test environment).

Treat pipeline reliability as a primary metric: the pipeline’s own uptime and accuracy directly affect delivery velocity. Monitor pipeline duration and failure rates; a pipeline that fails 30% of the time due to flaky tests erodes trust and leads to bypass attempts.
Security Pipeline Concepts – Tiered Enforcement

Security cannot be a single stage at the end; it must be layered. The requirement “Tiered pipeline enforcement is mandatory” reflects a defence‑in‑depth approach where scans occur at multiple touchpoints: pre‑commit hooks, CI on pull requests, post‑build scans, and runtime monitoring. This tiered model ensures that a failure at any level blocks the artefact or triggers an alert.
Static Analysis vs Dynamic Analysis
<ul>

• Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Analyses source code, bytecode, or binary without executing it. Tools like Semgrep, Bandit, or SonarQube scan for patterns of insecure coding (SQL injection, hardcoded credentials, misconfigurations). SAST is fast, can be run on every pull request, and catches entire vulnerability classes early. However, it can produce false positives and cannot detect runtime‑dependent issues.

• Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): Analyses a running application, typically in staging, by sending malicious payloads and observing responses. OWASP ZAP or Burp Suite can find cross‑site scripting (XSS), injection flaws, and configuration errors that SAST misses because they depend on the actual deployment environment (e.g., a misconfigured web server). DAST is slower and must be run against a live instance; thus it fits well later in the pipeline (post‑staging deploy).

Combined approach: SAST blocks PRs; DAST runs in staging and, if it finds critical issues, can block promotion to production or trigger an urgent fix alert. Neither is sufficient alone; they complement each other.
Secret Detection Lifecycle – Pre‑Commit vs CI
Secrets (API keys, tokens, passwords) must never enter source control. The detection lifecycle uses multiple layers:
<ol>

• Pre‑commit hooks: Tools like detect-secrets, git-secrets, or talisman scan staged files locally and refuse to commit if a secret pattern is found. This is the fastest and cheapest defence, but it’s optional—developers might skip --no-verify. Hence, it is only the first tier.

• CI pipeline scanning: Even if a secret slips past the pre‑commit hook, the CI pipeline must scan the entire repository history and the staged changes using tools like truffleHog (which scans for high‑entropy strings and known patterns) or GitGuardian. This stage must be blocking: if a secret is found, the pipeline fails and notifies the security team. Critical: Once a secret is pushed to a remote repository, it must be considered compromised immediately—revoke it, rotate it, and investigate.
• Post‑push monitoring: Even after a PR is merged, continuous secret scanning (GitHub Advanced Security, GitLab Secret Detection) can alert if a secret appears in any historical commit.
Implementation: In a GitHub Actions workflow, add a step that runs truffleHog against the latest commit. If it finds any verified secret, exit with a non‑zero code.

# Example: TruffleHog in CI
- name: Secret Detection
  run: |
    # Scan git diff of HEAD
    trufflehog filesystem . --json | tee secret-report.json
    # Exit if any secret found
    if grep -q '"Verified":true' secret-report.json; then
      echo "Secrets detected! Pipeline blocked."
      exit 1
    fi
Image Vulnerability Scanning vs Runtime Security
<ul>

• Image vulnerability scanning: Performed before deployment on the built container image. Trivy (open source, fast) or commercial tools like Snyk scan the OS packages and application dependencies for known CVEs. This is a blocking stage in the pipeline before the image is pushed to the registry or deployed. It answers: “Does this image contain unpatched critical vulnerabilities?”

• Runtime security: Monitors containers while they run in production. Tools like Falco, Aqua, or Datadog Runtime Security detect anomalous behavior (e.g., unexpected network connections, privilege escalation, reading sensitive files). This complements image scanning because vulnerabilities can be introduced after deployment (e.g., a zero‑day in a library), or an attacker might exploit a misconfiguration not visible in the static image.

Image scanning is a gate; runtime security is a guardrail. Both are essential. The CI pipeline must enforce image scanning with a severity threshold (e.g., block on HIGH and CRITICAL). Runtime security alerts go to the on‑call team for immediate investigation.
False Positives vs Acceptable Risk Thresholds
Security tools produce noise. Ignoring all findings is dangerous; blocking on every low‑severity alert kills velocity and encourages bypass. The solution is a risk‑based triage:
<ul>

• Define severity thresholds: CRITICAL and HIGH vulnerabilities must block the pipeline. MEDIUM creates a Jira ticket for the next sprint. LOW is tracked as a backlog item or ignored if in a non‑reachable component.

• Manage false positives: Maintain a suppression file for each tool (e.g., .trivyignore, .semgrepgignore) with explicit justification and an expiration date (e.g., “false positive in libX: not exploitable, review by 2025‑06‑01”). All suppressions must be approved via PR, ensuring visibility.

• Contextual risk: A CRITICAL CVE in a library used only in a test helper is not a production risk. Use vulnerability tools that support reachability analysis, or manually assess. If it’s truly unreachable, suppress with a clear note.

# Example .trivyignore file
# CVE-2024-1234 - False positive: vulnerability affects only Windows, our base image is Alpine
CVE-2024-1234
# CVE-2024-5678 - Suppressed until fixed upstream; no known exploit vector
# Expiry: 2025-09-01
CVE-2024-5678
Review the suppression list monthly in a dedicated meeting to ensure exceptions remain valid and are not permanent.
Blocking vs Non‑Blocking Stages
Not all stages need to block the pipeline. The decision is based on risk and maturity:
<ul>

• Blocking stages: Must pass for the artefact to proceed. Examples: unit tests, build success, image scan with severity threshold, SAST with custom critical rules, secret detection. These are your iron gates.

• Non‑blocking stages: Run in parallel and report results, but a failure does not stop progression. They provide early warning and detection. Examples: DAST scan (initially), license compliance checks, performance tests (often non‑blocking unless they exceed a critical SLO). Over time, non‑blocking stages can be promoted to blocking once their false‑positive rate is acceptably low and operational processes are in place.

Pipeline design pattern: Use conditional logic to allow non‑blocking failures to generate tickets or alerts without failing the build. For example, in GitHub Actions, use continue-on-error: true and then evaluate the output in a later step to decide if an alert should be sent.

# Example: non-blocking DAST
- name: DAST scan
  continue-on-error: true
  run: zap-full-scan.py -t http://staging/$APP -r zap-report.html
- name: Notify if DAST found high issues
  if: always()
  run: |
    if grep -q 'High' zap-report.html; then
      echo 'High severity DAST findings; ticket created.'
      # call Jira API
    fi
Direct from requirement: Tiered pipeline enforcement is mandatory.

This means you must have multiple enforcement levels: pre‑commit for developers, CI for pull requests, post‑deploy scans, and runtime monitoring. No single stage is sufficient; defence in depth is the only way to keep pace with threats. The pipeline’s architecture must reflect this by orchestrating checks at different lifecycle phases, each with appropriate blocking behaviour, and ensuring that evidence from each tier is centrally logged for audit purposes (see Module 14).

modules.title: Module 3 — Kubernetes & GKE Autopilot
content: Kubernetes is the operating system of the cloud, but GKE Autopilot reduces its operational surface so you can focus on production discipline, not cluster administration. This module discards enterprise complexity and zeroes in on the practical knowledge required to run multi‑environment, production‑grade workloads safely and cost‑effectively. You will learn to think in terms of namespaced isolation, precise resource budgeting, health‑probe design that avoids cascading failures, deployment strategies that minimise user impact, and Ingress configuration for real‑time traffic. The goal is to treat Kubernetes as a reliable platform, not a pet project.
Declarative Infrastructure Model

Kubernetes operates on a declarative model: you describe the desired state of your workloads (Deployments, Services, Ingresses) in YAML or JSON manifests. Controllers continuously reconcile the actual state to match the desired state. This is fundamentally different from imperative scripting—you do not tell Kubernetes “start a container, then another”; you declare “I want three replicas of this pod running at all times”. The control plane ensures it stays that way, even if nodes fail or pods crash.

Production implication: You never manually kubectl exec into a pod to “fix” something—you update the manifest and apply it. This enforces Infrastructure as Code, audit trails, and repeatable deployments. Every change to the cluster is version‑controlled via Git, and the pipeline applies the manifests. Ad‑hoc fixes are replaced by disciplined rollouts.
Practical workflow:
<ol>
• Write a Deployment YAML (deployment.yaml) with container image, resource requests, probes.
• Apply it: kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml (or via Helm).
• If you need to update, change the YAML (e.g., new image tag) and re‑apply. Kubernetes performs a rolling update automatically.
• If you need to fix a pod, you never edit it directly; you adjust the Deployment and let the controller recreate the pods.
This approach eliminates configuration drift—every environment configuration is exactly what’s in Git, and kubectl diff can show inconsistencies before applying.
Pods vs Deployments vs Services – Real Purpose, Not Definitions
It’s easy to recite textbook definitions; the real value is understanding their operational roles:
<ul>

• Pod: The smallest deployable unit—one or more tightly coupled containers that share a network namespace and storage volumes. A pod is ephemeral; it can be killed and rescheduled. You rarely create bare Pods; you manage them through higher‑level resources. The pod’s IP changes on restart, which is why you need a Service.

• Deployment: The controller that manages a ReplicaSet, ensuring a specified number of pod replicas are running. It provides rolling updates—when you change the pod template, it creates new replicas and terminates old ones according to strategy parameters. In production, your stateless services are almost always Deployments (or StatefulSets for stateful workloads).

• Service: A stable network endpoint that selects pods based on labels. It provides a fixed ClusterIP (and optionally a LoadBalancer IP) and load‑balances traffic to ready pods. Because pods come and go, the Service decouples consumers from specific pod IPs. This is essential for blue‑green deployments—the Service selector can be switched to point to a new set of pods.

Real‑world design: A typical microservice consists of a Deployment (managing pods) and at least one Service (exposing those pods). If you need to expose the service externally, you also have an Ingress. The Deployment’s pod template contains the application container with its resource requests, probes, and environment variables. The Service selects pods via labels like app: myapp.

# Minimal example: Deployment + Service
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: chat-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: chat-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: chat-api
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/project/chat-api:abc123
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 3000
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 3000
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: chat-api
spec:
  selector:
    app: chat-api
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 3000
Namespace Isolation Strategy (Dev/Staging/Prod)

Namespaces partition a single Kubernetes cluster into virtual clusters. They are the primary boundary for multi‑environment (dev, staging, prod) setups when you want to share a cluster (common in Autopilot for cost efficiency). Proper isolation prevents a noisy dev workload from crashing prod.
Isolation layers:
<ul>

• Logical separation: Create at least three namespaces: dev, staging, prod. Each contains independent Deployments, Services, and ConfigMaps. You can run separate versions of the same service side‑by‑side.

• ResourceQuota: Assign per‑namespace quotas to cap total CPU, memory, and number of objects. This ensures a runaway process in dev cannot starve prod of resources. For Autopilot, quotas also prevent cost overruns.
• NetworkPolicy: Define policies to restrict traffic between namespaces. For example, only allow traffic from prod to the database, while dev cannot reach it. This enforces security boundaries.
• RBAC: Use role‑based access control so that developers only have write access to dev namespace, and only platform team can deploy to prod. This ties back to CI/CD enforcement.

# Namespace creation with labels for cost tracking
kubectl create namespace prod --labels='environment=production,team=platform'

# Example ResourceQuota for dev namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-quota
  namespace: dev
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "10"
    requests.memory: 20Gi
    limits.cpu: "20"
    limits.memory: 40Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "10"

In Autopilot, the cluster’s underlying nodes are managed for you; the namespace becomes your primary unit of cost and capacity control. Because Autopilot charges per pod request, quotas directly map to your budget.
Resource Requests vs Limits – Cost vs Stability Tradeoff

Kubernetes distinguishes between requests (guaranteed minimum) and limits (maximum burst). This distinction is critical for both stability and cost, especially on Autopilot where the bill is based on requests.
<ul>

• Request: The amount of CPU/memory reserved for the container. The scheduler uses requests to decide which node can host the pod. If a node lacks the requested resources, the pod stays pending. Requests are what you pay for in Autopilot—every requested CPU and GiB of memory incurs a fixed per‑second cost.

• Limit: The hard cap on resource consumption. CPU beyond the limit is throttled (not killed), but memory beyond the limit results in an OOMKill (container termination). Limits must be set high enough to accommodate load spikes without over‑provisioning cost.
Cost/stability trade‑off:
<ul>
• If you set requests too low, the scheduler may pack too many pods on the same node, causing performance degradation and OOMKills when workloads spike. Your service becomes unstable.
• If you set requests too high, you pay for unused capacity, especially in Autopilot where every millicore is billed.

A common safe starting pattern: observe actual usage under typical peak load for a week, and set requests to the 95th percentile usage. Then set limits to 2x–3x the request for CPU (allow bursts) and maybe 1.5x–2x for memory (because OOMKill is more dangerous). Always run Vertical Pod Autoscaler in recommendation mode (not auto‑apply) to gather data and refine requests over time.

# Autopilot pricing is based on requested resources; this pod costs:
# CPU: 500m * $0.0445/hour = ~$0.022/hour (pricing varies)
# Memory: 512Mi * $0.00495/hour = ~$0.0025/hour
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "1000m"
    memory: "1Gi"

In Autopilot, the node pool is invisible; you pay per pod‑second, so rightsizing is a direct cost engineering activity (see Module 9). Use monitoring tools (Cloud Monitoring) to track actual vs requested over time.
Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA) Behavior Tuning

HPA automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics (e.g., requests per second). In production, you must tune its behavior to prevent thrashing and ensure smooth scaling.
<ul>

• Target average utilization: Typically 70% CPU. This means if the average CPU across pods exceeds 70% of their request, HPA adds pods. Don’t set too high (risk of overload) or too low (unnecessary scaling).
• Scale‑down stabilisation window: How long HPA waits before removing pods after a scale‑down recommendation. Set to e.g., 300 seconds (5 minutes) to avoid rapid flapping when traffic oscillates.

• Scale‑up policies: How many pods can be added per minute. Use a step size (e.g., add at most 2 pods every 60 seconds) and a maximum rate. This prevents a sudden traffic spike from scaling the cluster into capacity limits too fast.

# HPA with behaviour tuning (Kubernetes 1.27+)
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: chat-websocket
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: chat-websocket
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 50
        periodSeconds: 60
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 2
        periodSeconds: 60

For WebSocket workloads, CPU isn’t always the best metric—connections are memory‑bound. Use custom metrics like active_connections from Prometheus/Cloud Monitoring via the custom metrics adapter. This ensures you scale based on the actual capacity limit (connection count) rather than CPU, which might be idle with many sockets.

Autopilot interaction: Autopilot manages the underlying nodes; HPA just creates pods. Because Autopilot can add nodes transparently, you can set a higher maxReplicas without worrying about node provisioning. However, your cost will increase linearly, so still set an upper bound.
Rolling Updates vs Blue‑Green vs Canary
Kubernetes supports multiple deployment strategies; choosing the right one minimises risk and user impact.
<ul>

• Rolling Update (default): Gradually replaces old pods with new ones. You control the pace with maxSurge and maxUnavailable. It’s in‑place and simple, but if the new version has a latent bug, some users will hit errors until you roll back. Use for stateless services with fast health checks and low risk.

• Blue‑Green: Deploy a completely separate replica set (green) alongside the existing one (blue). After green passes health checks, switch the Service selector or Ingress rule to point to green. Instant rollback by switching back. Requires double capacity during cutover, but gives you a safe instant cutover. Similar to a full deployment swap, you need to handle database migrations carefully (green must be backward‑compatible).

• Canary: Deploy a small number of new pods (e.g., 5% of total) and route a fraction of traffic to them. Monitor error rate and latency; if fine, gradually increase the canary percentage until 100%. This is the safest method because you expose real users gradually and can abort early. In Kubernetes, canary is often implemented via multiple Deployments with a shared Service and an Ingress that uses header‑based or weight‑based routing (if using a service mesh like Istio, or GKE’s Gateway with weighted traffic splitting).
Strategy guidelines:
<ul>
• Use rolling update for low‑risk internal services (dev, staging).
• Use blue‑green for production deployments where instant rollback is critical and cost of double capacity is acceptable.
• Use canary for mission‑critical production services where you want to test with real traffic and abort automatically.
In Autopilot, all strategies work identically; the underlying node management is handled for you.
Health Checks – Liveness vs Readiness vs Startup Probes
Health probes determine a pod’s lifecycle. Misconfigured probes can cause cascading failures; correctly designed probes make deployments seamless.
<ul>

• Readiness probe: Indicates whether the pod can serve traffic. When it fails, the pod is removed from the Service endpoints. Use for warm‑up (e.g., cache loading), dependency checks (database connectivity), or graceful draining before shutdown. A pod should never receive traffic if its readiness fails. This protects users from failed instances.

• Liveness probe: Indicates whether the pod is alive. If it fails repeatedly, Kubernetes restarts the container. Critical caution: A liveness probe that depends on external services (database, Redis) can cause a restart storm if those backends are temporarily unavailable. Always design the liveness endpoint to check only internal health (e.g., “is the process alive?”) and not downstream dependencies. A lightweight /healthz that returns 200 if the event loop is running suffices.

• Startup probe: Used for slow‑starting containers (legacy apps, heavy preload). Once the startup probe succeeds, liveness and readiness take over. This prevents liveness from killing the container prematurely. Set a longer initial delay and failure threshold.
Example probe configuration:

readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /ready
    port: 3000
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 3000
  initialDelaySeconds: 15 # after readiness pass
  periodSeconds: 20
  failureThreshold: 3
startupProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /startup
    port: 3000
  initialDelaySeconds: 0
  periodSeconds: 5
  failureThreshold: 30 # up to 150s startup

WebSocket‑specific considerations: A WebSocket server readiness probe can be an HTTP endpoint that returns 200 if the server is ready to accept upgrades. The liveness probe must not close connections. It should simply report if the server process is alive. The startup probe can wait for Redis connections to be established.

Always test probe behavior under load: if the readiness probe fails during a traffic spike, pods are removed from the Service, increasing load on remaining pods, potentially causing a cascade. Mitigate with a higher failure threshold and a slower reaction.
Ingress Architecture – L7 Routing, TLS Termination

The Ingress resource defines how external HTTP(S) traffic reaches your Services, typically via an L7 load balancer (HTTP(S) Load Balancing on GKE). For real‑time WebSocket applications, Ingress configuration must be carefully tuned.
<ul>

• TLS termination: Always terminate TLS at the load balancer (or Ingress controller). Use Google‑managed certificates or cert‑manager for automatic renewal. The connection from Ingress to backend pods can be HTTP (within the private cluster) or you can re‑encrypt (TCP+SSL) for defence in depth. For production, terminating at the edge is sufficient performance‑wise and simplifies cert management.

• L7 routing: The Ingress can route based on hostname (api.example.com) and path (/api/*, /ws/*). For WebSocket, you need to ensure the backend service timeout is set high enough (default 30s is too low). On GKE Ingress, you create a BackendConfig with a longer timeoutSec:

# BackendConfig for WebSocket endpoints
apiVersion: cloud.google.com/v1
kind: BackendConfig
metadata:
  name: websocket-backendconfig
spec:
  timeoutSec: 3600  # 1 hour
  connectionDraining:
    drainingTimeoutSec: 300
Reference this BackendConfig in your Service annotation:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  annotations:
    cloud.google.com/backend-config: '{"default": "websocket-backendconfig"}'
...

Additionally, enable session affinity (Client IP) only if you use sticky sessions for WebSocket; otherwise, if you rely on Redis pub/sub, you can use round‑robin. The Ingress must be able to handle the WebSocket upgrade header—GKE Ingress supports it natively.
ConfigMaps vs Secrets – Separation of Config vs Sensitive Data
Kubernetes provides ConfigMaps for non‑sensitive configuration and Secrets for credentials. The separation is not just a label; it has security implications.
<ul>

• ConfigMap: Stores configuration files, environment variables, or command‑line arguments. Data is plain text. Use for feature flags, allowed origins, log levels, etc. They can be mounted as files or env vars.

• Secret: Intended for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys). Data is base64‑encoded (not encrypted by default; you must enable encryption at rest on the API server). In GKE Autopilot, secrets are encrypted at rest by default using a Google‑managed key. However, the stronger practice is to use Workload Identity to pull secrets from Secret Manager at runtime, avoiding Kubernetes Secrets for long‑lived credentials entirely (see Module 6).

Best practice: Never store raw secrets in ConfigMaps. Use Kubernetes Secrets only for short‑lived, auto‑rotated credentials or as a mounting interface for external secret stores. For static application configuration, use ConfigMaps; for sensitive data, use Google Secret Manager with Workload Identity so pods fetch the latest value at startup.

# ConfigMap for non-sensitive config
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  log_level: "info"
  allowed_origins: "https://example.com,https://app.example.com"
This separation ensures that a developer who accidentally logs a config map doesn’t expose secrets, and that secret rotation does not require a ConfigMap update.
Cluster Cost Dynamics – Autopilot vs Standard
Understanding how GKE Autopilot charges is essential to avoid sticker shock and to make informed architectural decisions. Autopilot flips the billing model from per‑node to per‑pod.
<ul>

• Autopilot billing: You pay only for the compute resources your pods request (CPU, memory, and ephemeral storage). There is a small per‑cluster management fee. You do not see or pay for the underlying nodes; Google manages them. This means if you over‑request resources, your bill inflates proportionally. Conversely, if you set tight requests, your costs stay low. Autopilot also charges for outbound traffic (egress) and additional managed services.

• Standard cluster billing: You provision and manage node pools (VMs), and you pay for those VMs plus associated resources (disk). If you have idle capacity, you still pay for it. You can pack pods efficiently to maximise utilisation, but you bear the operational burden of node management.

Cost impact of resource requests: In Autopilot, every millicore of CPU request incurs cost even if the pod is idle. So you must right‑size aggressively. The default requests in many Helm charts are too high—review them. Use Vertical Pod Autoscaler recommendations or manual monitoring to set realistic values.

Edge cases: Autopilot enforces minimum resource requests (e.g., 250m CPU, 512Mi memory per container) and may revise your requests upward to meet its node sizing constraints. This can increase cost unexpectedly. Always check after deploy: kubectl describe pod to see if resources were modified.
Cost optimisation mindset:
<ul>
• Use HPA to scale down to minimum replicas during low‑traffic hours (cron‑based scaling).
• Set namespaces with ResourceQuotas to prevent uncontrolled pod creation.
• Monitor cost per service using labels and billing exports (BigQuery).
• For batch jobs (background workers), use GKE or Cloud Run; Cloud Run can be more cost‑effective if jobs are sparse.
In summary, Autopilot aligns cost with usage, making cost engineering a first‑class activity. The discipline of precise resource requests directly translates to profit margins.
Not enterprise complexity — production discipline

This module has focused on the essential Kubernetes concepts that are directly applicable to operating a real‑time, multi‑tenant SaaS with GKE Autopilot. There is no need for custom controllers, in‑house service meshes, or bespoke node configurations. By mastering these core primitives—declarative manifests, namespacing, probes, HPA tuning, and Ingress configuration—you achieve a production‑grade platform that is both simple and robust. The discipline is in consistently applying these patterns and iterating on resource usage based on real data, rather than building a Rube Goldberg machine.

modules.title: Module 4 — WebSocket Scaling + Redis
content: Real‑time features—chat, live notifications, collaborative editing—require persistent, stateful connections. In a single‑server world, WebSocket handling is straightforward: accept connection, keep socket open, push data. In production, you run multiple server instances behind a load balancer, and suddenly a message generated on instance A must reach a user connected to instance B. Redis becomes the spine that synchronises state across the entire WebSocket fleet. This module dives deep into the operational realities: connection lifecycle, upgrade handling, scaling patterns, delivery guarantees, backpressure, and graceful draining. By the end, you’ll understand why Redis is load‑bearing and how to design your real‑time layer to survive outages, deploys, and traffic spikes without dropping critical messages.
Stateful Connections in Distributed Systems

Each WebSocket connection is a dedicated TCP socket bound to a specific server process. This is fundamentally stateful: the server holds a reference to the client, tracks its authenticated identity, and may have in‑flight messages. In a horizontally scaled system, the load balancer distributes new connection requests to any available replica. Without additional coordination, a replica only knows about clients connected to itself. A message intended for a specific user may arrive at a server that does not hold that user’s connection.

This creates the classic “WebSocket scale problem”: how do you deliver a message to a user when you don’t know which server (if any) the user is connected to? The answer is to decouple connection ownership from message routing. Each server becomes a “dumb” forwarder: it accepts messages from clients, publishes them to a shared medium (Redis), and subscribes to the medium to push messages to locally connected clients. This turns a stateful connection problem into a stateless routing problem, with Redis as the stateful hub.

Key insight: The WebSocket server’s state (open sockets) is local and ephemeral; the system’s source of truth for messages is the database. Redis bridges the gap with real‑time fan‑out. This design allows you to treat WebSocket servers as “cattle” that can be replaced—provided you drain them properly.
Connection Lifecycle Management
A WebSocket connection goes through distinct phases, each requiring specific handling in production:
<ol>

• Upgrade request: The client sends an HTTP request with Upgrade: websocket headers. The server (or Ingress) must authenticate the request before upgrading. This is the ideal place to reject unauthenticated or rate‑limited clients with an HTTP 401 or 429, avoiding wasteful socket allocation.

• Connection established: After the upgrade, the server assigns the connection a unique ID and maps it to a user/tenant. It subscribes the connection’s handler to the relevant Redis channels (user‑specific, tenant‑specific, channel‑specific). A heartbeat mechanism (ping/pong) starts to detect stale connections.

• Active communication: The server receives client frames, validates payload size and rate, and publishes well‑formed messages to Redis. It also listens to Redis and pushes incoming messages to the client. Backpressure logic (see below) kicks in if the client cannot keep up.
• Graceful close: Either side initiates the close handshake (close frame). The server unsubscribes from Redis, removes the connection from local tracking, and logs the event.

• Abrupt disconnect: If the TCP socket drops (e.g., client network loss), the server detects it via heartbeat timeout or TCP RST. The same cleanup occurs, but there may be undelivered in‑flight messages (handled by reconciliation).
Every state transition must be monitored (metrics like connection openings, closures, abortive closes). Abrupt disconnects should not crash the server; they’re normal in mobile/browser environments.
WebSocket Upgrade Handling at Ingress

Before a connection reaches your WebSocket server, it passes through the load balancer / Ingress controller. This layer must be configured to support the WebSocket protocol, which deviates from normal HTTP in two ways: the Upgrade: websocket header, and the fact that the connection remains open for a long time.
<ul>

• Timeout configuration: Most load balancers have an idle timeout (e.g., 30s default). If no frame is sent, the connection is silently closed. For WebSocket, you must increase the backend service timeout. On GKE Ingress, this is done via a BackendConfig with timeoutSec: 3600 (or higher). On Nginx Ingress, the annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: "3600" is required. Without this, clients experience mysterious disconnections every 30 seconds.

• Connection draining: When a load balancer is instructed to drain connections (during a deploy or scale‑down), it stops sending new connections to the backend and waits for existing connections to close. You must set a draining timeout that exceeds your expected graceful shutdown period. For WebSocket, GKE BackendConfig’s connectionDraining.drainingTimeoutSec should be set to 300 or more, matching your server’s graceful shutdown timeout.

• Session affinity (optional): If you use sticky sessions, you need the Ingress to set a session cookie. However, if you use Redis pub/sub, you can avoid sticky sessions entirely, which simplifies horizontal scaling and resilience. We’ll explore this trade‑off next.

# GKE BackendConfig for WebSocket endpoints (timeout + draining)
apiVersion: cloud.google.com/v1
kind: BackendConfig
metadata:
  name: websocket-config
spec:
  timeoutSec: 3600
  connectionDraining:
    drainingTimeoutSec: 300
Horizontal Scaling Challenges – Sticky Sessions vs Pub/Sub
Two primary patterns exist for distributing WebSocket traffic across multiple replicas:
<ul>

• Sticky Sessions (Session Affinity): The load balancer inspects a cookie or uses client IP hashing to route all requests from a particular client to the same backend pod. This means once a client upgrades, all its subsequent frames go to the same pod. Simpler internally—no need for a backplane. However, if that pod dies (crash, deploy), the client is disconnected and must reconnect, losing any in‑flight state. Sticky sessions also create hotspots if some clients are much more active than others. They tie clients to a specific instance, making blue‑green deployments more disruptive.

• Pub/Sub Backplane (Redis): The load balancer uses simple round‑robin or least‑connections without affinity. Each WebSocket server subscribes to the Redis channels relevant to its connected clients. When any server receives a client message, it publishes to Redis. All servers subscribed to that channel receive the message and forward it to their locally connected clients. This decouples clients from any specific pod. A pod can be restarted without clients losing connection (they reconnect to another pod). It enables smooth deploys if you drain connections first (see draining section).

Production systems should default to the pub/sub pattern. It aligns with the cattle‑vs‑pets philosophy: WebSocket servers become interchangeable. The price is that Redis becomes a critical dependency that must be highly available. The next sections detail how to build this architecture robustly.
Redis Pub/Sub Fan‑out Architecture

Redis Pub/Sub is a fire‑and‑forget messaging system: messages are delivered to all currently subscribed clients but are not stored. This makes it extremely fast but also ephemeral—if a subscriber is not connected at the moment of publish, it misses the message. For real‑time notification (“chat message arrived”), this is acceptable because the durable source of truth is the database. The Redis pub/sub layer is only for live delivery.
Channel design: Use structured channel names that allow granular subscription. For a chat application, you might have:
<ul>
• tenant:{tenant_id}:channel:{channel_id} — messages in a specific channel.
• tenant:{tenant_id}:user:{user_id} — notifications directed to a user (mentions, DMs).
• presence:channel:{channel_id} — online/offline status changes.

When a client connects, the server subscribes to the channels relevant to that client (using PSUBSCRIBE or multiple SUBSCRIBE commands). When a message arrives from the client, the server validates it and publishes to the appropriate channel.

# Python pseudo-code for WebSocket server using redis-py async
import redis.asyncio as redis

r = redis.Redis(host='redis-master', port=6379)
pubsub = r.pubsub()

async def handle_client(websocket, user_id, tenant_id, channel_id):
    # Subscribe to relevant channels
    await pubsub.subscribe(f'tenant:{tenant_id}:channel:{channel_id}')
    await pubsub.subscribe(f'tenant:{tenant_id}:user:{user_id}')
    
    # Start listener task
    listener = asyncio.create_task(redis_listener(websocket, pubsub))
    
    # Wait for client messages
    try:
        async for message in websocket:
            # Parse, validate, and publish
            await r.publish(f'tenant:{tenant_id}:channel:{channel_id}', message)
    finally:
        listener.cancel()
        await pubsub.unsubscribe()
        await websocket.close()
The Redis subscriber is a dedicated event loop that reads messages and calls websocket.send(). This pattern is the heart of fan‑out.

Redis cluster considerations: Pub/Sub in a Redis Cluster works, but messages are only propagated to nodes that have subscribers for the channel. If you have a large cluster, you may need to ensure all nodes are connected. Alternatively, use a dedicated Redis instance (non‑clustered) for pub/sub and a separate instance for caching. For most SaaS workloads, a single Redis instance (plus replica for failover) can handle hundreds of thousands of messages per second.
Message Delivery Guarantees – At‑Most‑Once, At‑Least‑Once

Redis pub/sub provides at‑most‑once delivery: if a subscriber is temporarily disconnected, messages published during that gap are lost. This is why you must separate the live delivery path (Redis) from the durable storage path (PostgreSQL). A typical chat system works as follows:
<ol>
• Client sends message via HTTP POST to the API.
• API inserts message into PostgreSQL and returns the saved message (with a monotonic ID or timestamp) to the client. This provides immediate confirmation.
• API then publishes the message to Redis for live delivery.
If the WebSocket delivery fails (network, Redis down), the message is still in the database. Clients that reconnect can fetch the history and catch up.

For scenarios where at‑least‑once delivery is required (e.g., you cannot afford to miss a live message even if Redis hiccups), you can use Redis Streams with consumer groups. A stream stores messages durably (appended) and consumers acknowledge after processing. Unacknowledged messages are redelivered. However, this adds latency and complexity. For most chat and notification use cases, at‑most‑once with database reconciliation is the pragmatic balance.

# At-least-once with Redis Streams (conceptual)
# Producer
await r.xadd('stream:tenant:123:messages', {'msg': message_json})

# Consumer group
await r.xgroup_create('stream:tenant:123:messages', 'websocket-group', 0, mkstream=True)

# Consumer reads
messages = await r.xreadgroup('websocket-group', 'consumer1', {'stream:tenant:123:messages': '>'}, count=10)
for msg in messages:
    await send_to_websocket(msg)
    await r.xack('stream:tenant:123:messages', 'websocket-group', msg['id'])
Reconnection Reconciliation Strategies

When a client disconnects and reconnects (due to network change, server restart, or deploy), it needs to catch up on any messages missed during the gap. The reconciliation strategy is critical for user experience:
<ol>

• Client stores a cursor: After the WebSocket connection is upgraded, the client sends a connect frame containing the ID of the last message it processed (e.g., a monotonically increasing sequence number or timestamp). The server queries the database for messages with IDs greater than that cursor and sends them immediately.
• Server pushes catch‑up: Once the backlog is drained, the server subscribes to the live Redis channels. New messages are delivered in real‑time.

• Idempotency: The client may receive a message it already saw (e.g., if the reconnect happened just after a delivery but before the client’s cursor was updated). Clients must be idempotent: use the message ID to deduplicate.
This hybrid approach gives the illusion of persistent connection without missing a beat. It crucially decouples the real‑time push from the connection state.

# Server-side reconciliation on WS connect
async def on_connect(websocket, user_id, channel_id, last_cursor):
    # Fetch missed messages from DB
    rows = await db.fetch('SELECT * FROM messages WHERE channel_id = $1 AND id > $2 ORDER BY id', channel_id, last_cursor)
    for row in rows:
        await websocket.send(row.to_json())
    # Now subscribe to live updates
    await pubsub.subscribe(f'channel:{channel_id}')
Backpressure Handling

If the client network is slow, the WebSocket server’s send buffer fills up. The server pushes messages faster than the client can consume, leading to unbounded memory consumption and potential OOM. This is backpressure.
Strategies to handle backpressure:
<ul>

• High‑water mark: Each connection tracks the number of bytes in its send queue. When it exceeds a threshold (e.g., 64KB), the server stops reading from the upstream (Redis or network) for that connection. It may drop non‑critical messages (typing indicators, presence) and keep only essential ones.

• Flow control via TCP: The operating system’s TCP socket buffer naturally applies backpressure to the server’s application layer. When the buffer is full, websocket.send() will block or raise an error. The server must handle this gracefully—pause consumption for that connection, not for all connections.

• Connection drop: If the send buffer stays at the high‑water mark for too long (e.g., 30 seconds), the server should close the connection with a descriptive code (1013) and log a warning. A slow client is harming the whole system; it must reconnect.
Implement per‑connection buffering with an asyncio-friendly pattern that yields control when the buffer is full, using non‑blocking sends and an in‑memory queue bounded by size.
Connection Draining During Deploys
When you deploy a new version of the WebSocket server, Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to the old pods. You must not abruptly kill connections; instead, you gracefully drain them:
<ol>
• SIGTERM received: The server immediately marks itself as not ready (readiness probe fails), so the load balancer stops routing new connections to it.

• Send close frames: For each connected client, send a WebSocket close frame with a special status code (e.g., 1012 – Service Restart) and a message “Server is restarting, please reconnect”. Many WebSocket libraries automatically reconnect when they receive 1012.

• Wait for delivery of pending messages: Allow a configurable period (e.g., 25s) for any in‑flight messages (from Redis) to be sent to clients. Do not accept new messages from Redis after SIGTERM (unsubscribe).

• Exit: After the draining window, close remaining sockets and exit. The terminationGracePeriodSeconds in Kubernetes should be longer than this total drain time (e.g., 300s) to allow the process to finish.

# Graceful shutdown in Node.js WebSocket server
const server = require('http').createServer();
const wss = new WebSocket.Server({ server });

process.on('SIGTERM', () => {
  console.log('SIGTERM received, draining connections...');
  wss.clients.forEach(client => {
    client.close(1012, 'Server restart');
  });
  server.close(() => {
    process.exit(0);
  });
  // Force exit if draining takes too long
  setTimeout(() => process.exit(1), 25000);
});
This pattern ensures zero‑downtime deploys: clients reconnect to new pods, which immediately start listening on the same Redis channels and push any missed messages via reconciliation.
SIGTERM Handling for Graceful Shutdown – The Full Pipeline
Graceful shutdown is not just a server concern; it must align with Kubernetes and the load balancer. The full coordination:
<ul>

• Pre‑stop hook: Optionally, use a preStop lifecycle hook in the Pod spec to sleep 5 so the iptables rules update before the process receives SIGTERM. This prevents new connections from arriving after draining begins.

• Readiness probe failure: When SIGTERM is received, the server returns 500 on the readiness endpoint, causing the Service to remove the pod from endpoints. This takes a few seconds (depending on how often the probe is checked).
• Drain logic: As described above.

• Termination grace period: Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds to, say, 300 seconds. This gives the server enough time to drain connections and exit cleanly. If the server hasn’t exited by then, Kubernetes sends SIGKILL.
Test this repeatedly in staging: deploy, kill a pod, and verify that clients reconnect and catch up without duplicate messages.
Critical rule: Redis is load‑bearing, not optional.
Redis is the backbone of real‑time delivery. Without it, WebSocket servers cannot coordinate. Its availability directly impacts your ability to push live updates. Therefore:
<ul>

• Use a highly available Redis setup. In GCP, use Memorystore for Redis with a standard tier (failover replica) and enable high availability (Sentinel). The instance must automatically failover within seconds.
• Monitor memory usage, connection count, and replication lag. Memory exhaustion in Redis will cause it to evict keys or stop accepting writes, crashing your pub/sub.
• Do not use Redis pub/sub for messages that absolutely must persist; always persist to the database first, then publish. This way, Redis can fail and be restarted without permanent data loss.

• Plan for Redis being down: the system should still accept HTTP writes (API persists to DB) and respond to clients with diagnostics: “Live updates temporarily unavailable, please refresh.” The WebSocket servers should reconnect to Redis automatically when it comes back.

• Consider using a connection pool for Redis subscriptions; each subscriber connection counts against the maxclients limit. If you have many WebSocket pods, ensure maxclients is set high enough (Memorystore default is 65,000).

Treat Redis as a tier‑1 service with the same operational care as your primary database. Its failure mode is rapid: message delivery stops instantly. Build runbooks (Module 12) for Redis outage that include graceful degradation and fast recovery.

modules.title: Module 5 — Database + Supabase -> Production Thinking
content: A production SaaS application lives and dies by its database. Supabase provides a PostgreSQL database with a rich set of features – Row Level Security, real‑time subscriptions (Realtime), managed APIs, and storage – but using them correctly requires production thinking, not quick‑start assumptions. This module goes deep into database‑layer tenant isolation, query optimization for hot tables, indexing strategies, connection pooling, safe migration patterns, the boundary between built‑in Realtime and custom WebSocket services, secure storage access, and the fundamental trade‑offs between consistency and performance. By the end, you will design a database layer that is fast, secure, and ready to scale.
Row Level Security (RLS) Design Patterns

Row Level Security is a PostgreSQL feature that restricts which rows a user can see or modify, enforced at the database level. Supabase relies heavily on RLS to implement multi‑tenant isolation and user‑specific permissions. A missing or misconfigured RLS policy can lead to catastrophic data leaks – and because the enforcement is in the database, even a bug in the application layer won’t bypass it.
How RLS Works

RLS is enabled per table with ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. Once enabled, all rows are invisible by default (for all roles except the table owner and bypass roles). Policies define conditions under which rows can be accessed or modified. Policies can be permissive (additive) or restrictive (intersected).
