• Rollback: To revert an infra change, revert the Terraform commit and re‑apply. Because of state, it will restore previous configuration quickly.

By adopting Terraform in this manner, you gain the superpower of deterministic, reproducible infrastructure. Your entire cloud footprint becomes a versioned artifact, just like your container images. This is production discipline in infrastructure, and it underpins all the other modules in this course.

modules.title: Module 14 — ISO / Audit Thinking
content: Compliance frameworks like ISO 27001, SOC 2, and HIPAA are not merely paperwork burdens—they are evidence that your system operates with consistent, verifiable security and quality controls. Auditors do not trust your word; they demand timestamped, tamper‑proof records that prove every change was authorised, tested, and deployed securely. This module reframes your engineering practices through the lens of auditability. You will learn to design systems that automatically generate the audit trail: PR history, deployment logs, security scan reports, and change management records. By embedding evidence collection into the CI/CD pipeline and infrastructure as code, compliance becomes a natural by‑product of good engineering, not a frantic scramble before an audit. At the highest level, we adopt the mindset: evidence is part of system design.
Evidence‑Driven Engineering

Evidence‑driven engineering means that for every operation that affects security, availability, or data integrity, the system produces a durable, verifiable record. This record must answer the auditor’s fundamental questions: Who made the change? What exactly was changed? When did it happen? How was it approved and tested? Why (linked to a ticket or requirement)? Without embedded evidence, you will rely on after‑the‑fact manual collection, which is error‑prone and time‑consuming.

To achieve evidence‑driven engineering, you must design your pipeline, infrastructure, and workflows to produce immutable, cryptographically verifiable artifacts at each stage. These artifacts are stored in a dedicated, retention‑locked GCS bucket (or similar) with strict access controls. Only the automation service account can write; humans have read‑only access. The bucket has a lifecycle policy that retains objects for the period required by the framework (e.g., 7 years for ISO 27001).

The guiding principle: if it’s not recorded, it didn’t happen. This applies to code merges, deployments, configuration changes, security scans, incident responses, and even access grants. Everything that touches production must leave a trace.
Audit Trail Generation: The Core Components
An audit trail is a chronological set of records that reconstruct a sequence of events. In a modern SaaS delivery pipeline, the audit trail can be generated automatically from the following sources:
1. Pull Request (PR) History

The PR is the birthplace of change. It contains the diff of the code, the reviewers’ comments, the approvals, and the CI checks that passed. Tools like GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket automatically preserve this history. To make it audit‑ready:
<ul>
• Branch protection: Require that every merge to the release branch (e.g., main) is done via a PR. No direct pushes. This guarantees that every change has a corresponding PR record.
• Required approvals: Enforce at least one (ideally two) approvals from designated code owners. The PR record shows who approved and when.
• Status checks: All CI stages (tests, linting, SAST, dependency audit, image scan) must pass and be recorded as status checks on the PR. The pipeline itself becomes an evidence‑generating engine.
• Link to issue tracker: Include the Jira/Rally ticket ID in the PR description or branch name. This connects the change to the business justification. The ticket itself provides the “why”.

For audit evidence, you can export the PR metadata (title, author, approvals, merged timestamp, associated commits) to a log file that is stored alongside the build artifact. For example, a CI job can call the GitHub API to fetch the PR details and dump them as a JSON file, then archive it. Even without export, the SCM provider retains history, but exporting ensures you have a copy under your control.
2. Deployment Logs
Every time an artifact is promoted to an environment (dev → staging → production), a deployment record must be created. This record includes:
<ul>
• The exact artifact version (container image SHA, build ID).
• The environment to which it was deployed.
• Timestamp of deployment start and completion.
• Deployment result (success/failure) and logs.
• Who approved the deployment (if manual gate was required).

In Kubernetes, the act of applying a new image tag creates a new ReplicaSet and records an event. You can store the kubectl rollout history output as evidence. Better, use a CI/CD system that logs every deployment action. For example, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or Cloud Build have built‑in job logs. The deployment job should output structured JSON with the above fields and push it to the evidence bucket.
A practical approach: use a “deployment notifier” script that runs after a successful production deploy. The script creates a JSON record like:

{
  "event": "deployment",
  "artifact": {
    "image": "us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/proj/chat-api@sha256:abc123",
    "commit": "def4567",
    "build_id": "build-789"
  },
  "environment": "production",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "approved_by": "jane.doe@example.com",
  "pipeline_url": "https://github.com/myorg/chat/actions/runs/123456"
}
This record, stored in the evidence bucket, provides an immutable trace. It can be queried to show auditors exactly what code is running in production at any given time.
3. Security Scan Reports

Security scans (SAST, dependency audit, image scan, DAST) are a critical part of a secure SDLC. Each scan produces a report. For audit purposes, you must capture not just the pass/fail status but the raw report output, because auditors may ask for the specific CVEs that were found and how they were triaged.
Generate a report for each scan type per build:
<ul>
• Semgrep: output JSON or SARIF, store alongside build artifacts.
• Dependency audit: save the audit log (e.g., npm audit --json).
• Trivy: output SARIF or JSON (e.g., trivy image --format sarif).
• ZAP: HTML/JSON report.

Store these reports in the evidence bucket with a naming convention like scans/{commit_sha}/{scanner_name}-report.json. Then, the pipeline can generate a summary that references these reports, which the approval gate can display. For compliance, you must also show that critical/high findings were addressed. If a finding was suppressed via a .trivyignore file, the justification and approval must be evident. This can be a separate “exception log” stored alongside.
Automated archiving of scan reports means that when an auditor asks, “Show me the scan results for the release deployed on March 15th,” you can instantly retrieve them.
Change Management Traceability

Frameworks like ISO 27001 require that changes to production systems are managed, approved, and traceable. This means you must demonstrate a clear link from a change request through to the deployment and verification. The pipeline is the enforcer of this traceability.
Implement the following links:
<ul>

• Ticket → PR: Use naming conventions (feat/JIRA-1234) and reference the ticket in the PR body. Automatically validate that the PR references a valid ticket (e.g., via a GitHub Action that checks the description).

• PR → Build: The CI pipeline is triggered by the PR merge. The build must record the PR number and commit SHA. The build artifact (image) is tagged with the commit SHA, creating an unbreakable chain.
• Build → Deployment: The deployment job records which artifact was deployed, and the pipeline run URL provides the full log of the build and deploy stages.
• Deployment → Monitoring: Post‑deploy smoke tests and canary analysis produce logs that prove the deployment was successful. These logs are attached to the deployment record.

The complete traceability chain enables an auditor to start from a production pod and, within minutes, identify the exact code version, who wrote it, who approved it, which security scans it passed, and which ticket authorised it. This is not a manual spreadsheet—it’s generated by the pipeline. For example, a tool like syft or the image metadata already contains the git SHA, and you can query the CI system to retrieve the rest.

Implementation tip: In your CI/CD system, define a standard build metadata structure that is passed through stages. Use it to tag images and annotate logs. Store a build manifest (build-info.json) in the evidence bucket that aggregates all identifiers.
Compliance‑Ready Workflows
Instead of preparing evidence manually during audit season, design your workflows so that compliance is a by‑product of daily operations. This involves:
<ul>

• Automated evidence collection: As described above, every pipeline run generates a set of artifacts (scan reports, deployment logs, PR metadata). A dedicated stage at the end of the pipeline pushes these artifacts to the retention bucket. This runs every time, not just before an audit.

• Periodic attestation generation: Create a weekly (or daily) job that scans the evidence bucket, extracts key compliance metrics (e.g., average time to approve PRs, number of failed security scans, deployment frequency), and compiles a compliance dashboard or PDF report. For ISO 27001, you need to demonstrate continuous improvement—these metrics serve as input to management review.

• Automated control testing: Many controls can be verified automatically. For example, a script can check that all production deployments were made via the CI pipeline by querying the Kubernetes audit logs and comparing them with the deployment records in the evidence bucket. Any deviation triggers an alert (and points to a potential click‑ops violation). This “continuous control monitoring” closes the loop.
Consider a concrete example: a compliance workflow for a new production deployment. The pipeline will:
<ol>
• Record the PR merge and its associated ticket.
• Run all security scans and store reports, failing if any violate policy.
• Build and push the image, storing the image digest.
• Deploy to staging, run integration tests, store results.
• Prompt for manual approval (which is logged with timestamp and approver identity).
• Deploy to production and store the deployment log.
• Run smoke tests and attach the outcome.

All this evidence is automatically archived. At the end of the quarter, the compliance officer simply runs a script that assembles all deployments with their evidence links into a single signed report. Auditors receive read‑only access to the evidence bucket and can independently verify each record. This transforms audits from a multi‑week fire‑drill into a non‑event.
Security Documentation Discipline

Beyond the automated evidence, frameworks require documentation of policies, procedures, and system descriptions. The traditional approach is a set of Word documents on a shared drive, which quickly become stale. In a DevOps culture, security documentation should be treated as code: version‑controlled, reviewed, and continuously updated.
Documentation as Code
<ul>

• Store in Git: Keep all security policies (e.g., access control policy, incident response plan, change management policy) in a docs/ folder within the main repository or a dedicated compliance repo. They are Markdown or AsciiDoc files.
• Review and approval: Any change to a policy requires a PR with designated approvers (e.g., CISO, head of engineering). The PR itself becomes evidence of review.
• Versioning: Use tags or releases to mark approved versions of the policy set (e.g., v1.2). This provides a clear history.

• Linking to evidence: Within the documentation, reference the automated tools and evidence locations. For example, the “Access Control Policy” can state that IAM bindings are managed via Terraform in infra/modules/iam. Auditors can see the policy and then verify the Terraform code and state.

This discipline ensures that documentation is always current because it's part of the engineer’s daily workflow. When the architecture changes, the corresponding doc update is part of the same PR. A CI check can enforce that any PR modifying a service code also requires a corresponding doc change if needed (via code ownership).
The Principle: Evidence Is Part of System Design
From day one, you must design for auditability just as you design for scalability. This means:
<ul>
• Structured logs with trace IDs: Every log entry carries the context needed to trace it back to a request, a deployment, and a tenant. This enables rapid forensics.
• Immutable build artifacts: Build once, store forever (or at least per retention policy). Never delete images or build logs within the retention window.
• Automatic evidence archiving: The pipeline is the only path to production, and every pipeline run archives its evidence. There is no deployment without evidence.
• Cryptographic integrity: Where possible, sign artifacts (e.g., using Sigstore/cosign to sign container images) so that provenance can be verified independently.

By embedding evidence generation into the system (rather than retrofitting it), you not only reduce compliance cost but also improve internal discipline. The same records that satisfy an auditor help your on‑call engineers debug incidents faster. For example, the deployment log links directly to the commit and scan reports, enabling the responder to immediately know what changed and whether it was secure.

In summary, “evidence is part of system design” means that you cannot claim a feature is done until it produces its audit trail. Each user story includes acceptance criteria like “deployment of this service must generate an immutable deployment record.” This shifts the cultural mindset: compliance becomes a feature, not a roadblock.
